proposals.title: Engineering Voltage via Chemical Inductance: Decoupling Open-Circuit Voltage from Electron Lifetime in Perovskite Memristive Systems
proposals.motivation: In conventional solar cells and electrochemical systems, the open-circuit voltage (Voc) is intrinsically linked to and limited by the electron recombination lifetime, a fundamental constraint on energy conversion efficiency. This coupling arises from the capacitive nature of charge storage and recombination dynamics. Decoupling Voc from lifetime would enable new pathways to exceed traditional voltage limits, a significant challenge in photovoltaics and neuromorphic electronics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by introducing a dominant, voltage-generating chemical inductance—a non-Faradaic process where ion dynamics create an inductive phase shift—in a perovskite-based memristive cell, the Voc can be governed by the inductive time constant rather than the capacitive recombination lifetime.
proposals.method: We will fabricate mixed ionic-electronic perovskite (e.g., MAPbI3) thin-film devices with engineered interfaces and mobile ion profiles to enhance chemical inductance, as quantified by Bisquert's 'chemical inductor' model. Using voltage-step chronoamperometry and impedance spectroscopy (10 mHz to 1 MHz), we will extract the equivalent circuit parameters, specifically the inductive loop magnitude and time constant (τ_L = L/R). We will systematically correlate τ_L with Voc under illumination, independently varying recombination lifetime via trap-density modulation (chemical treatment) and chemical inductance via ionic composition (e.g., halide mixing).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental demonstration of a photovoltaic/memristive system where Voc is primarily set by an inductive mechanism. It will deliver a new design principle for voltage generation in electrochemical systems, potentially impacting high-voltage perovskite solar cells and biologically realistic artificial neurons where signal timing (inductance) is decoupled from loss mechanisms (recombination).
proposals.novelty: While the chemical inductor concept is known, its active use to dictate the quasi-Fermi level splitting and thus Voc, breaking the canonical lifetime-voltage relationship, is a radically novel application. This moves inductance from a parasitic element to a functional, governing component.

proposals.title: Deconvolving Transport and Recombination in Perovskite Neuristors via Warburg-Adapted Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: The development of perovskite-based neuromorphic devices, or neuristors, requires precise control over their analog memory and dynamical properties, which are governed by ionic-electronic coupling. However, standard impedance models for perovskites, like the chemical inductor, often conflate charge transport and recombination processes, limiting the ability to engineer specific temporal responses. Adapting advanced deconvolution methods from battery research, where Warburg diffusion and charge transfer are routinely separated, can provide the necessary resolution to link specific electrochemical resistances to neural-like computing functions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the complex impedance of volatile perovskite resistive switching devices can be quantitatively decomposed into distinct Warburg-like transport and recombination resistance elements, and that their voltage-dependent evolution directly dictates the device's capacitive/inductive memory window and synaptic plasticity.
proposals.method: We will perform voltage-dependent electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) on methylammonium lead iodide perovskite neuristor devices. The impedance spectra will be analyzed using a modified transmission line model incorporating a Gerischer element, adapted from lithium-ion battery analysis for mixed ionic-electronic conductors. Specific fitting will use the ZView software with a complex non-linear least squares (CNLS) algorithm to deconvolve the series resistance, charge transfer (recombination) resistance at the electrode/perovskite interface, and the chemical diffusion impedance (Warburg component) related to ion migration and bulk recombination.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new quantitative framework for analyzing perovskite neuromorphic devices, directly linking measurable electrochemical parameters to device dynamics. It will yield design rules for tailoring the timescale and strength of synaptic plasticity by controlling specific transport and recombination pathways. The methodology will bridge the fields of solid-state electrochemistry and neuromorphic engineering.
proposals.novelty: While EIS is standard in photovoltaics and batteries, its application with advanced battery-specific deconvolution models to perovskite-based neuromorphic devices is novel. This approach moves beyond descriptive equivalent circuits to a physically grounded separation of processes that are intentionally entangled in neuristor operation.

proposals.title: Mapping and Quantifying Sub-Bandgap Electronic States in TiO2 Photoanodes via Surface Photovoltage Spectroscopy and Impedance Analysis
proposals.motivation: The performance of TiO2-based photoelectrochemical cells for water splitting is critically limited by sub-bandgap electronic states that act as recombination centers. Pre-2026, these states are often inferred indirectly from electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) or transient photocurrents, but a direct, spatially resolved spectroscopic mapping of their energetic distribution and density is lacking. This gap hinders the rational design of surface passivation strategies and the optimization of charge extraction.
proposals.hypothesis: Surface photovoltage spectroscopy (SPS), when combined with impedance-derived Mott-Schottky analysis, can directly and quantitatively map the energetic distribution and density of sub-bandgap states in TiO2, correlating specific state signatures with chemical capacitance and recombination losses observed in EIS.
proposals.method: We will fabricate planar and nanostructured TiO2 photoanodes. SPS measurements will be performed using a Kelvin probe in a controlled atmosphere, scanning monochromatic light from above-bandgap to deep sub-bandgap energies to generate surface photovoltage (SPV) spectra. Concurrently, we will perform intensity-modulated photovoltage spectroscopy (IMVS) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) under controlled bias and illumination to extract the chemical capacitance and recombination resistance. The SPS spectra will be deconvoluted using a modified analysis that treats sub-bandgap transitions as perturbations to the surface band bending, and the derived state densities will be cross-validated against the voltage-dependent chemical capacitance from EIS.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, quantitative map of TiO2 bandgap states, moving beyond phenomenological equivalent circuit elements. It will establish a novel experimental protocol combining SPS and EIS to decouple capacitive (state filling) and resistive (recombination) contributions of sub-bandgap states. This will offer a fundamental tool for diagnosing performance bottlenecks in metal oxide photoelectrodes and perovskite-TiO2 interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While SPS and EIS are established separately, their combined, quantitative use to deconvolute and map sub-bandgap states in TiO2 photoanodes is novel. The approach directly links the spectroscopic signature of a state (from SPS) to its electrochemical function as a chemical capacitor or recombination center (from EIS), which is non-obvious from either technique alone.

proposals.title: Energy-Dependent Density of States in Nanostructured Perovskites: Revisiting the Chemical Capacitance Model for Neuromorphic and Photoelectrochemical Systems
proposals.motivation: The chemical capacitance (C_μ) is a cornerstone concept for analyzing electrochemical and optoelectronic systems, from perovskite memristors for neuromorphic computing to semiconductor photoanodes for water splitting. Its standard derivation assumes a uniform density of states (DOS), which is likely invalid for nanostructured, disordered, or surface-engineered materials where the DOS can vary strongly with energy. This gap limits the accuracy of impedance spectroscopy interpretations for state-of-the-art devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating a strongly energy-dependent DOS, such as exponential or Gaussian distributions common in disordered semiconductors, will fundamentally alter the voltage and frequency dependence of the measured chemical capacitance, providing a more accurate physical description of charge storage and dynamics in perovskite-based electrochemical systems.
proposals.method: We will derive a generalized C_μ expression by integrating a non-uniform DOS, g(E), into the chemical potential calculation for electrons/holes. The model will be tested by fitting impedance spectroscopy data from two systems: (1) volatile perovskite resistive switching devices (analog memory) and (2) nanostructured hematite or bismuth vanadate photoanodes. The analysis will use a modified Mott-Schottky approach and equivalent circuit modeling with a voltage-dependent constant phase element (CPE) to capture the distributed kinetics resulting from the DOS dispersion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a revised framework for interpreting impedance spectra in materials with disordered energy landscapes, directly impacting the analysis of neuromorphic device operation and charge transfer in photoelectrochemical cells. It will link the 'chemical inductor' phenomenon and anomalous capacitive dispersions to specific features in the underlying DOS.
proposals.novelty: While DOS effects are studied in traditional semiconductors, their explicit incorporation into the chemical capacitance model for dynamic electrochemical systems like perovskite memristors and water-splitting photoanodes is novel, moving beyond the standard uniform-DAS assumption prevalent in pre-2026 analyses.

proposals.title: Deconvoluting Recombination Pathways in Perovskite Solar Cells via Frequency-Resolved Chemical Capacitance Analysis
proposals.motivation: In perovskite solar cells and other nanostructured semiconductors, the chemical capacitance (C_μ) is a key parameter reflecting the density of states and Fermi level position. However, its direct, quantitative link to specific recombination mechanisms (e.g., bulk SRH, surface, or ionic-mediated recombination) remains ambiguous. Clarifying this link is critical for rational device optimization, as current impedance models often treat recombination resistance and C_μ as independent, obscuring their fundamental kinetic coupling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the voltage and frequency dependence of the measured chemical capacitance can be decomposed to quantify the contribution of distinct recombination pathways, with each pathway imprinting a characteristic signature on the capacitive response.
proposals.method: We will fabricate methylammonium lead iodide perovskite solar cells with controlled interfacial modifications. Using impedance spectroscopy (IS) and intensity-modulated photovoltage spectroscopy (IMVS) across a broad frequency range (1 mHz to 1 MHz) and under varying bias/illumination, we will extract the chemical capacitance (C_μ) and recombination resistance (R_rec). We will develop a dynamical systems model based on chemical inductor concepts, where recombination pathways are represented by parallel RC elements with distributed time constants. Numerical deconvolution of the C_μ(f, V) spectra using a regularization algorithm will be performed to isolate the capacitive contribution of each pathway.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new analytical framework to directly assign measured capacitance features to physical recombination processes. It will yield quantitative rate constants for competing loss channels, moving beyond phenomenological equivalent circuits. The methodology will be broadly applicable to other electrochemical and optoelectronic systems where chemical capacitance is central.
proposals.novelty: While C_μ is widely used, its intentional deconvolution to kinetically resolve recombination is novel. Combining the 'chemical inductor' framework with multi-frequency analysis to dissect C_μ represents a significant advance over static Mott-Schottky or single-time-constant interpretations.

proposals.title: A Unified Dynamical Model for Perovskite Optoelectronic Devices: Transient Photoresponse Governed by Chemical Capacitance and Inductive Ion Dynamics
proposals.motivation: The dynamic operation of perovskite solar cells and memristors is governed by slow ionic motion and electronic charge accumulation, which are not fully captured by standard equivalent circuit models. This limits the understanding and optimization of transient phenomena like photovoltage decay, current overshoot, and analog memory switching, which are critical for stability and neuromorphic applications. A time-dependent model integrating the chemical capacitance—central to describing the density of states occupancy—is urgently needed to bridge the gap between impedance spectroscopy and real-time device operation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the transient photovoltage and photocurrent in perovskite devices can be quantitatively modeled by a coupled electrochemical system where the chemical capacitance, representing the change of chemical potential with charge, is the key element governing the time-dependent Fermi level, and that inductive ion migration provides the necessary feedback for oscillatory and memory phenomena.
proposals.method: We will develop a time-domain numerical model based on the chemical capacitance formalism (C_chem = q^2 (∂n/∂E_F)), coupled with drift-diffusion equations for electronic and ionic carriers. The model will be implemented using a finite-difference scheme in MATLAB/COMSOL, solving the coupled Poisson, continuity, and ion migration equations. Specific voltage/light excitation protocols (e.g., small- and large-signal voltage steps, light pulses) will be simulated to generate transient currents and voltages, which will be directly compared to experimental measurements from perovskite solar cells and resistive switching devices. The impedance spectrum will be derived from the time-domain model via Fourier transform for validation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles, time-dependent framework to interpret transient measurements in perovskite optoelectronics, directly linking the chemical capacitance concept to observable device kinetics. It will establish a design rule for optimizing response times and analog memory retention in neuromorphic devices. The model will unify the description of solar cells and memristors within a single electrochemical neuron-like framework.
proposals.novelty: While chemical capacitance is used in impedance analysis, its explicit integration into a full time-domain device model for transient photoresponse is novel. The approach uniquely combines Bisquert's 'chemical inductor' concept with the chemical capacitance to describe inductive loops and memory effects dynamically, moving beyond the frequency-domain interpretation.

proposals.title: Mapping Chemical Capacitance and Ionic Dynamics in Perovskite Solar Modules via Distributed Impedance Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: The transition from single perovskite solar cells to large-area modules introduces complex, scale-dependent electrochemical phenomena that are poorly understood. Pre-2026, the chemical capacitance—a key descriptor of ion accumulation and electronic density of states in perovskite devices—has been characterized only at the cell level. Its manifestation and impact on voltage distribution, hysteresis, and stability in interconnected modules, where lateral ionic and electronic transport couple, remains a critical knowledge gap for reliable upscaling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the distributed chemical capacitance across a perovskite module, influenced by local ionic dynamics and interconnectivity, creates non-uniform voltage biases and frequency-dependent losses that limit module efficiency and exacerbate performance degradation under operational bias.
proposals.method: We will fabricate 5x5 cm2 perovskite minimodules with series-interconnected sub-cells. A multi-channel impedance analyzer will perform spatially resolved impedance spectroscopy (IS) by contacting individual sub-cells and the full module. We will apply the chemical inductor analysis framework and Mott-Schottky interpretation, developed by Bisquert's group, to deconvolute the chemical capacitance (Cμ) and transport resistance from IS spectra at different bias voltages and illumination intensities. A distributed equivalent circuit model, incorporating neuron-like electrochemical elements for each sub-cell, will be fitted to the spatial IS data to map ionic and electronic coupling.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first experimental framework for module-level electrochemical impedance analysis, directly linking macroscopic performance losses to microscopic ionic processes. It will provide design rules for module architectures and encapsulation that mitigate detrimental ionic redistribution, directly informing the stability roadmap for perovskite photovoltaics.
proposals.novelty: While chemical capacitance is well-studied in single cells, applying this concept to diagnose spatial electrochemical inhomogeneity and coupling in operational modules is a novel, systems-level approach. The integration of neuron-inspired dynamical models with perovskite IS data to describe module-scale behavior represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Electrochemical Impedance Modeling of Fermi-Level Coupling and Chemical Capacitance in Multi-Absorber Tandem Solar Cells
proposals.motivation: Tandem solar cells, especially those using nanostructured perovskite absorbers, promise efficiencies beyond the Shockley-Queisser limit. However, their complex, voltage-dependent interfacial energetics, governed by chemical capacitance (C_μ), are poorly understood. A predictive model for C_μ in multi-absorber stacks is critical to optimize charge extraction and minimize voltage losses, accelerating the development of high-efficiency, low-cost photovoltaics.
proposals.hypothesis: The total chemical capacitance in a series-connected, nanostructured tandem cell is not a simple series addition of individual absorber capacitances but is governed by a coupled, voltage-dependent Fermi-level alignment that can be modeled as an equivalent electrochemical circuit with internal chemical inductors.
proposals.method: We will develop a drift-diffusion model extended with Fermi-level statistics to describe carrier populations in each absorber. The voltage-dependent chemical capacitance, C_μ(V)=q²(dn/dE_F), will be calculated for each layer and their coupling. Using impedance spectroscopy analysis analogous to Bisquert's 'chemical inductor' framework, we will derive a general equivalent circuit for the tandem stack. The model will be validated against impedance data from perovskite-perovskite and perovskite-silicon tandem cells from pre-2026 literature.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first dedicated analytical framework for impedance and capacitance analysis of multi-absorber tandem cells, linking nanoscale carrier statistics to macroscopic device performance. It will deliver design rules for minimizing interfacial voltage losses and identify characteristic impedance signatures for diagnosing bottlenecks in emerging tandem architectures.
proposals.novelty: While chemical capacitance is well-studied in single absorbers, its behavior in coupled, voltage-graded multi-absorber systems is unexplored. The application of an electrochemical systems approach, including inductive effects from carrier relaxation, to model tandem cell dynamics represents a significant conceptual advance beyond standard equivalent circuit models.

proposals.title: Quantifying Surface State Distributions in Perovskite Memristors via Frequency-Resolved Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: In perovskite-based resistive switching devices and photoanodes, surface states critically govern voltage-driven dynamical behavior, analog memory, and chemical inductance effects. However, standard techniques like Mott-Schottky analysis provide only indirect, averaged estimates of surface state density (Nss), lacking the energetic resolution needed to link specific states to device kinetics. A direct spectroscopic measurement of Nss(E) is essential to model and engineer these electrochemical systems for neuromorphic and energy applications.
proposals.hypothesis: The density and energetic distribution of surface states in metal-halide perovskite interfaces can be directly quantified by deconvoluting the frequency-dependent chemical capacitance (Cμ) from the total measured impedance, revealing a direct correlation between specific surface state energies and the inductive/relaxation dynamics observed in volatile memristive switching.
proposals.method: We will employ advanced frequency-resolved electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) on perovskite resistive switching devices and model photoanodes. The method centers on extracting the chemical capacitance (Cμ) component from the total impedance (Z(ω)) across a broad frequency range (10 mHz – 10 MHz) under varying DC bias. The derivative dCμ⁻¹/dV will be calculated, and the surface state density Nss(E) will be directly obtained via Nss(E) = (q² A)⁻¹ * dCμ⁻¹/dV, where q is electron charge and A is area. This analysis will be coupled with voltage-pulse dynamical testing to correlate specific Nss(E) features with relaxation time constants.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, energy-resolved maps of surface states in perovskite memristors, moving beyond phenomenological equivalent circuits. It will establish a quantitative, spectroscopy-based framework to link surface state distributions to device-level phenomena like analog memory retention, volatile switching rates, and chemical inductance, thereby advancing the design of bio-inspired electrochemical systems.
proposals.novelty: While EIS is common, directly extracting the complete Nss(E) spectrum from Cμ in perovskite electronic devices, rather than semiconductor electrodes, is novel. This approach uniquely bridges Bisquert's established theories of chemical capacitance and inductive loops with concrete, quantitative surface state spectroscopy for neuromorphic materials.

proposals.title: Quantifying Surface State Hole Transfer Kinetics in Perovskite Photoanodes via Transient Photocurrent and Chemical Inductor Analysis
proposals.motivation: In perovskite-based photoelectrochemical cells for water splitting, surface states critically mediate charge transfer but also act as recombination centers, limiting efficiency. Pre-2026, the kinetic parameters of hole transfer from these states remain poorly quantified, hindering rational interface design. This gap is significant as it prevents a full impedance-based understanding of the voltage-dependent competition between charge transfer and recombination.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the voltage-dependent kinetics of hole transfer from surface states to the electrolyte can be directly extracted by combining transient photocurrent decay measurements with a chemical inductor circuit model, where the inductive loop in electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) originates from the slow charging/discharging of these states.
proposals.method: We will fabricate metal halide perovskite photoanodes and perform intensity-modulated photocurrent spectroscopy (IMPS) and small-perturbation transient photocurrent decay measurements under controlled bias. The transient data will be analyzed using a dynamical systems model that treats surface states as a chemical capacitance in series with a voltage-dependent charge transfer resistance. This model, inspired by the 'chemical inductor' concept, will be fitted to the photocurrent transients to extract the rate constants for hole transfer and recombination as explicit functions of applied voltage.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, kinetic map of surface state activity, linking transient optoelectronic measurements to the well-established impedance models used in the field. It will advance the interpretation of Mott-Schottky and impedance plots for perovskite photoanodes by deconvolving the surface state contribution. The methodology will offer a new diagnostic tool for engineering more efficient and stable photoelectrochemical interfaces.
proposals.novelty: While EIS and steady-state photocurrent measurements are common, the explicit use of transient photocurrent to parameterize the voltage-dependent kinetics of the chemical inductor mechanism for surface states is novel. This approach directly connects Bisquert's prior work on chemical inductors and perovskite capacitive memory to the dynamical analysis of photoelectrochemical kinetics.

proposals.title: Decoupling Surface State Dynamics in Perovskite Photoanodes via Intensity-Modulated Photocurrent Spectroscopy (IMPS) and Chemical Inductor Analysis
proposals.motivation: In perovskite-based photoelectrochemical cells for water splitting, surface states critically mediate charge transfer and recombination, yet their kinetic interplay remains poorly resolved. This ambiguity limits the rational design of efficient and stable photoanodes. Intensity-modulated photocurrent spectroscopy (IMPS) is a powerful tool for probing such dynamics but requires advanced models to deconvolve the complex impedance of these systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the IMPS response of perovskite photoanodes can be quantitatively modeled by incorporating a 'chemical inductor' element, representing the surface state occupancy dynamics, thereby allowing the decoupling of charge transfer and recombination rate constants.
proposals.method: We will fabricate methylammonium lead iodide (MAPI) perovskite photoanodes and perform IMPS measurements under controlled bias and illumination. The IMPS data (complex photocurrent vs. modulation frequency) will be analyzed using a newly developed equivalent circuit model. This model integrates a classical Randles circuit for charge transfer with a 'chemical inductor' branch, derived from the coupled differential equations for surface state filling/emptying, to explicitly represent the inductive loop observed in IMPS. Fitting will be performed using non-linear least squares algorithms (e.g., Levenberg-Marquardt) to extract the surface state density, electron capture coefficient, and hole transfer rate constant.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new quantitative framework for interpreting IMPS in perovskite photoelectrodes, directly linking measured spectra to fundamental surface kinetic parameters. It will advance the understanding of voltage-dependent surface state behavior in perovskite-based energy conversion systems, offering a pathway to optimize interfacial engineering for solar fuel generation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic application of Bisquert's 'chemical inductor' concept, previously developed for electronic devices, to the dynamic photoelectrochemical IMPS response of perovskites, creating a unified model for surface state kinetics that goes beyond conventional equivalent circuit analysis.

proposals.title: Surface State Dynamics and Chemical Inductance in Perovskite-Based Photocathodes for Proton Reduction
proposals.motivation: The analysis of surface states via impedance spectroscopy is crucial for understanding the kinetics and efficiency of photoanodes for water oxidation, as demonstrated in Bisquert's recent work on Mott-Schottky interpretation. However, a parallel, detailed investigation of surface states on photocathodes for the proton reduction reaction (e.g., for H2 evolution) is significantly less developed. This knowledge gap limits the rational design of efficient and stable integrated photoelectrochemical cells for solar fuel generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the surface states on perovskite-based photocathodes exhibit a dominant chemical inductance, analogous to that described for other electrochemical systems, and that this inductance governs the voltage-dependent kinetics and Faradaic efficiency of the proton reduction reaction.
proposals.method: We will fabricate perovskite (e.g., methylammonium lead halide) thin-film photocathodes with controlled surface chemistry. The central technique will be electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) under illumination and applied bias, analyzed using the impedance-derived capacitance and the chemical inductor framework. We will construct and fit a detailed physical model incorporating surface state capacitance, charge transfer resistance, and chemical inductance to the EIS data. Voltage-dependent rate constants will be extracted from the inductive loop characteristics and correlated with direct measurements of photocurrent and H2 evolution Faradaic efficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative impedance model for surface state dynamics on perovskite photocathodes, directly linking the chemical inductance phenomenon to catalytic performance. It will establish a diagnostic framework to deconvolute surface recombination from productive charge transfer, offering a path to optimize surface passivation and co-catalyst integration for solar fuel production.
proposals.novelty: While Bisquert's group has applied surface state analysis to photoanodes and described the chemical inductor in other contexts, applying this combined impedance and dynamical systems analysis specifically to the proton reduction reaction on emerging perovskite photocathodes is a novel and logical extension of their core methodologies.

proposals.title: Electrochemical Impedance Analysis of Ion Dynamics and Interfacial Chemical Capacitance in Large-Area Solution-Processed Perovskite Solar Modules
proposals.motivation: Scaling perovskite photovoltaics to large-area modules via solution deposition is hindered by spatial inhomogeneities in film quality and ionic distribution, which degrade performance and stability. Pre-2026, the link between scalable coating dynamics, the resulting electrochemical landscape, and module-level performance remains poorly understood, impeding manufacturing advancement.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the ionic-electronic dynamics and interfacial chemical capacitance, probed by impedance spectroscopy, are directly governed by the fluid-dynamic conditions of large-area coating, and that optimizing the ink formulation for specific coating methods can suppress detrimental chemical inductance and spatial voltage fluctuations.
proposals.method: We will develop slot-die and blade coating inks with tailored solvent systems and additives to control crystallization kinetics. Large-area films will be fabricated, and their spatial electrochemical properties mapped using impedance spectroscopy (IS) across the module substrate. The IS data will be interpreted using equivalent circuit models incorporating chemical capacitance and inductance, correlating fluid-dynamic parameters (shear rate, drying profile) with ionic diffusion coefficients, recombination losses, and the voltage-dependent capacitive response at the perovskite/transport layer interfaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a fundamental, electrochemically grounded framework for diagnosing and optimizing large-area perovskite coating processes. It will provide new design rules for ink formulation based on targeted ionic dynamics, directly linking manufacturing parameters to the electrical and capacitive signatures that define module performance and operational stability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying Bisquert's concepts of chemical capacitance and inductance—typically used for microscopic device analysis—to the macroscopic, spatially resolved problem of manufacturing-scale coating, creating a unique diagnostic tool for module upscaling.

proposals.title: Low-Temperature Electrochemical Luminescence as a Probe for Ion Dynamics and Interfacial States in Perovskite Light-Emitting Diodes
proposals.motivation: Perovskite LEDs (PeLEDs) suffer from efficiency roll-off and operational instability, which are strongly linked to ionic migration and interfacial charge accumulation. Pre-2026, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) has been used to study these phenomena, but it lacks direct correlation with luminescent output under dynamic electrical stress. Low-temperature electrochemiluminescence (ECL) offers a unique, simultaneous readout of electrochemical and optoelectronic processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying low-temperature ECL to PeLEDs will reveal distinct voltage-dependent luminescence regimes directly correlated with specific ionic relaxation times and interfacial capacitive states, providing a new fingerprint for device degradation pathways.
proposals.method: We will fabricate methylammonium lead iodide PeLEDs and integrate them into a cryogenic probe station. A custom voltage waveform generator will apply AC and DC bias sweeps while simultaneously measuring the ECL intensity and the complex impedance. The data will be analyzed using a modified Randles circuit model incorporating a chemical inductor element to account for ion-coupled electron transfer. The correlation between the imaginary part of the impedance (capacitance/inductance) and the phase-delayed ECL response will be mapped as a function of temperature (80-300 K) and voltage.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish low-temperature ECL as a novel diagnostic tool for perovskite optoelectronics, directly linking ionic kinetics to light emission efficiency. It will provide a dynamical systems framework to interpret voltage-driven hysteresis in PeLEDs, moving beyond static capacitance-voltage analysis.
proposals.novelty: While EIS is common, the simultaneous, phase-sensitive measurement of impedance and ECL at low temperatures in operating PeLEDs is novel. This approach directly merges Bisquert's expertise in chemical inductors and Mott-Schottky analysis with the operational study of light-emitting devices.

proposals.title: Electrochemical Impedance and Chemical Inductance in Low-Dimensional Perovskites: A Framework for Quantifying Nanoscale Dielectric Confinement
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, low-dimensional perovskites (nanocrystals, quantum dots) are promising for optoelectronics, but their unique dielectric confinement and restricted charge dynamics are poorly understood through standard impedance models. Juan Bisquert's recent work on 'Chemical Inductors' and capacitive/inductive characteristics in perovskite devices provides a novel framework to interpret these complex systems, bridging the gap between nanoscale photophysics and macroscopic electrical response.
proposals.hypothesis: The anomalous capacitive and inductive loops observed in impedance spectroscopy of low-dimensional perovskite structures directly arise from coupled ionic-electronic dynamics under strong dielectric confinement, and can be quantitatively modeled by extending the chemical inductor concept to include a geometry-dependent optical correction factor.
proposals.method: We will synthesize CsPbBr3 nanocrystals and 2D Ruddlesden-Popper perovskites with controlled dimensionality. Electrical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) will be performed over a wide frequency (1 mHz - 1 MHz) and voltage range. The data will be analyzed using the 'Generalized Poisson-Nernst-Planck' framework and equivalent circuit models featuring constant phase elements (CPEs) and chemical inductance. The optical correction factor, accounting for restricted emission angles in nanostructures, will be integrated into the model's dielectric constant to link confined photophysics to the measured chemical capacitance and inductance.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new interpretative tool for EIS in nanostructured perovskites, decoupling true charge transport from interfacial and capacitive artifacts. It will provide quantitative parameters linking nanoscale confinement to macroscopic impedance, directly impacting the design of perovskite-based neuromorphic devices and light-emitting diodes where analog memory and inductive effects are crucial.
proposals.novelty: While Bisquert's chemical inductor model has been applied to bulk films, its application to low-dimensional systems where dielectric and optical properties are severely modified by confinement is unexplored. The integration of an optical correction factor into an electrochemical impedance model for quantifying nanoscale ionic dynamics represents a significant conceptual advance.

proposals.title: Detailed-Balance Analysis of Perovskite LEDs: Quantifying Efficiency Limits via Angularly-Restricted Emission and Electrochemical Impedance
proposals.motivation: Perovskite light-emitting diodes (PeLEDs) have shown remarkable external quantum efficiencies (EQE), yet their fundamental limits and loss mechanisms remain debated. Pre-2026 detailed-balance models for solar cells, incorporating angular restriction, have been successfully adapted to perovskite photovoltaics, but a rigorous dual application to PeLEDs is lacking. This gap prevents a unified, physics-based framework linking the electrochemical and optical dynamics that govern PeLED performance, a core theme in Bisquert's research on voltage-driven systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the detailed-balance model, corrected for the angular distribution of emitted light and coupled with the device's electrochemical impedance, can accurately predict the ultimate efficiency and voltage-dependent EQE of PeLEDs, revealing the dominant non-radiative pathways.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Shockley-Queisser detailed-balance formalism for emission, integrating an angular restriction factor (étendue) specific to the PeLED's planar multilayer architecture. The model will be parameterized using experimental data (capacitance-voltage, electroluminescence spectra) from state-of-the-art perovskite cells. Crucially, we will incorporate the chemical inductor concept and impedance spectroscopy results to model voltage-dependent carrier recombination dynamics and capacitive charging effects, solving the coupled opto-electrochemical system numerically.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational efficiency-limit model for PeLEDs, distinguishing between intrinsic radiative and extrinsic non-radiative losses. It will establish a direct conceptual link between perovskite photovoltaic and light-emitting operation through impedance, offering a new diagnostic tool to interpret C-V and Mott-Schottky plots in the context of emission efficiency.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the dual application of a corrected detailed-balance model specifically to PeLEDs and its explicit integration with the electrochemical impedance framework characteristic of Bisquert's group, moving beyond purely optical models to a unified voltage-dependent dynamical systems description.

proposals.title: Electrochemical Impedance Analysis of a Quantum Dot Bulk Heterojunction Solar Cell: Decoupling Transport and Recombination in an Inverted Sensitization Architecture
proposals.motivation: In conventional quantum dot (QD) sensitized solar cells, the QD layer primarily acts as a light absorber and injector into a wide-bandgap electron transporter (e.g., TiO2), limiting charge transport efficiency and device thickness. An architecture where the QD layer itself serves as both the primary absorber and charge transporter could enhance light harvesting and carrier collection. This inversion of the sensitization concept remains poorly understood, particularly regarding its interfacial electrochemical dynamics and voltage-dependent capacitive effects, which are central to Juan Bisquert's research on impedance and chemical inductors in perovskite and photoelectrochemical systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in an inverted architecture with a mesoporous, electronically connected QD film as the primary transporter, the dominant charge storage and recombination pathways will shift from the QD/oxide interface to within the QD bulk and the QD/hole-transporter interface, manifesting as distinct features in the chemical capacitance and inductive loops in electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS).
proposals.method: We will fabricate solar cells using a mesoporous ZrO2 insulating scaffold infiltrated with a dense, sintered film of lead sulfide (PbS) QDs (with halide passivation) as the electron-transporting absorber, topped with a spiro-OMeTAD hole transporter. The core analysis will employ voltage-dependent impedance spectroscopy (0 to VOC, under dark and illumination) to construct capacitance-voltage (Mott-Schottky) and complex capacitance plots. We will apply Bisquert's Z-View and equivalent circuit models, specifically looking for voltage regimes where the chemical capacitance of the QD film dominates and where inductive loops (signifying chemical inductance from slow state dynamics) appear, correlating them with transient photovoltage decay measurements of recombination rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new physical model for charge accumulation and recombination in bulk-transport QD solar cells, directly extending the 'chemical inductor' concept to colloidal QD systems. It will establish design rules for inverted sensitization architectures by quantifying the trade-offs between enhanced absorption and bulk recombination losses, contributing to the fundamental understanding of electrochemical systems for energy conversion.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the advanced impedance interpretation frameworks developed for perovskites and dye-sensitized cells to a fundamentally different architecture where the sensitizer is the transporter, and in explicitly seeking the electrochemical 'fingerprints' (like chemical inductance) of this inverted operational mode, which has not been systematically investigated pre-2026.

proposals.title: Unified Impedance Model for Perovskite Memristive Systems: Coupling Ionic Migration Kinetics to Electronic Transport and Capacitive Phenomena
proposals.motivation: The dynamic interplay between ion migration and electronic transport underpins the operation of perovskite-based memristors, electrochemical cells, and neuromorphic devices, yet existing models often treat these processes separately. A comprehensive, coupled model is urgently needed to interpret complex impedance spectra, explain analog memory, and guide the design of next-generation devices with tailored dynamical responses. This directly addresses the challenge of understanding the 'chemical inductor' behavior and voltage-dependent capacitive features observed in volatile perovskite resistive switching.
proposals.hypothesis: The anomalous inductive and capacitive loops in perovskite memristor impedance spectra arise from a non-linear coupling between field-driven ion migration kinetics and the subsequent modulation of electronic charge transport and interfacial states. A unified model based on coupled differential equations can quantitatively reproduce these features and predict device dynamics.
proposals.method: We will develop a time-domain device model by coupling Poisson's equation, electronic drift-diffusion equations, and a kinetic equation for mobile ion density (e.g., vacancy migration) with a voltage-dependent diffusion coefficient. The model will be implemented in a finite-element framework (e.g., COMSOL or custom Python solver) to simulate current-voltage transients and small-signal impedance. Key parameters will be extracted by fitting simulated impedance spectra to experimental data from perovskite devices, using a complex non-linear least squares (CNLS) algorithm, with a focus on deconvolving the chemical capacitance, ionic transport resistance, and electronic recombination.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative framework to disentangle ionic and electronic contributions in mixed ionic-electronic conductors, moving beyond equivalent circuit analysis. It will deliver a predictive tool for designing perovskite-based neuromorphic devices with specific temporal dynamics (e.g., synaptic plasticity) and offer a fundamental interpretation of Mott-Schottky analysis in systems with significant ion migration.
proposals.novelty: While ionic and electronic models exist in isolation, the explicit, dynamical coupling of ion migration kinetics to both bulk electronic transport and interfacial chemical capacitance in a unified impedance model for perovskite memristors is novel. This approach directly tests the 'chemical inductor' concept as a emergent property of the coupled system, not just a circuit element.

proposals.title: Unifying the Ion Migration Mechanism in Perovskite Memristors: A Systematic Study of Halide Composition and Electrode-Dependent Hysteresis via Impedance Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: Metal halide perovskite memristors exhibit complex capacitive and inductive hysteresis in their current-voltage and impedance characteristics, which are central to their neuromorphic and analog memory functions. Pre-2026 work, including Bisquert's 'chemical inductor' model, attributes this to ion migration dynamics, but a systematic validation linking specific compositional changes (halide type) and electrode interfaces to the universality of this mechanism is lacking. Establishing this link is crucial for rational material design and reliable device operation.
proposals.hypothesis: The hysteretic 'chemical inductor' response in perovskite memristors is a universal signature of voltage-activated halide ion migration, and its characteristic timescales and amplitudes will scale predictably with halide anion mobility (I > Br > Cl) and the electrochemical activity of the contact material.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a matrix of perovskite (CsPbX3, X = I, Br, Cl, mixed) resistive switching devices with varied top/bottom electrodes (Au, Ag, ITO, graphene). Hysteresis will be characterized via voltage-sweep I-V measurements and analyzed through voltage-dependent electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) in the 0.1 Hz to 1 MHz range. The EIS data will be fitted using a generalized equivalent circuit incorporating constant phase elements and a chemical inductor (L_c) in series with a migration resistance (R_m), as per Bisquert's framework, to extract key dynamical parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive, quantitative map linking material composition and interfaces to the dynamical electrochemical response, validating the universality of the migration mechanism. It will deliver design rules for tuning hysteresis timescales for specific neuromorphic functions (e.g., short-term vs. long-term plasticity) and clarify the role of electrodes in modulating ion injection and interfacial reactions.
proposals.novelty: While ion migration is widely invoked, this study uniquely combines systematic halide and electrode variation with a rigorous impedance-based 'chemical inductor' analysis to decouple and quantify the contributions of bulk anion mobility and interfacial kinetics to the universal memristive hysteresis.

proposals.title: Quantifying Halide Ion Dynamics in Perovskite Memristors via Solid-State NMR: A Direct Link to Capacitive and Inductive Impedance Signatures
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Bisquert and others has revealed that metal halide perovskite memristors exhibit complex electrochemical impedance with capacitive and inductive loops, strongly suggesting coupled ion-electron dynamics govern resistive switching and analog memory. However, the direct measurement and quantification of halide ion (e.g., I⁻) movement under operational voltages, crucial for validating the 'chemical inductor' model, remains a significant challenge. Solid-state NMR offers a unique, element-specific probe for such dynamics but has been underutilized in this context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the voltage-driven, long-range diffusion and local hopping of halide ions (I⁻, Br⁻) in perovskite films, directly measurable via solid-state NMR relaxation and exchange spectroscopy, are the primary origin of the low-frequency inductive impedance and the capacitive memory effects observed in these devices.
proposals.method: We will fabricate MAPbI₃ and mixed-halide perovskite thin-film devices on NMR-compatible substrates. Using a Bruker solid-state NMR spectrometer, we will perform variable-temperature ¹²⁷I and ⁷⁹/⁸¹Br NMR experiments. Key techniques include: 1) Spin-lattice (T₁) and spin-spin (T₂) relaxation measurements to probe local ion mobility timescales. 2) 2D Exchange Spectroscopy (EXSY) to directly quantify ion exchange rates between distinct crystallographic sites or phase-separated domains under applied DC bias. 3) Line-shape analysis under in-situ electrical bias to correlate ion hopping rates with device impedance spectra (EIS) measured on identical samples.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative link between microscopic halide ion kinetics and the macroscopic electrochemical impedance of perovskite memristors. It will validate or refine the chemical inductor model for neuromorphic materials. The results will establish solid-state NMR as a critical diagnostic tool for ion dynamics in next-generation electrochemical memory and synaptic devices.
proposals.novelty: While NMR is used to study perovskite stability, its application to directly probe voltage-driven ion dynamics in operational memristive devices is novel. Combining in-situ NMR with impedance analysis to deconvolute the specific ionic contributions to inductive and capacitive signals represents a significant methodological advance beyond standard electrical characterization.

proposals.title: Spatially Resolving Ion Migration in Perovskite Layers via Transmission Line Impedance Models Adapted from Solid Oxide Fuel Cells
proposals.motivation: Ion migration in metal halide perovskites critically impacts device stability and hysteretic behavior in solar cells and memristors, but its spatial distribution within the active layer is poorly understood. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a powerful tool for probing ionic-electronic dynamics, yet standard lumped-element models lack spatial resolution. Adapting established depth-profiling EIS models from fuel cell research offers a novel route to map ion accumulation and transport gradients in perovskite films.
proposals.hypothesis: The distributed chemical capacitance and ionic resistance within a perovskite layer can be spatially resolved by modeling its impedance with a finite-length transmission line (TL) circuit, analogous to those used for porous electrodes in solid oxide fuel cells, revealing distinct ion migration profiles under bias.
proposals.method: We will fabricate perovskite (e.g., MAPbI3) devices with symmetric, ion-blocking electrodes for EIS measurements. Impedance spectra will be acquired under varying DC bias and analyzed using a finite-length Gerischer or TL model, where the chemical inductance (a Bisquert-group concept) and chemical capacitance are distributed along a transmission line representing the film thickness. The spatial distribution of the complex impedance will be extracted by fitting the model's characteristic length and time constants, using the ZView software with custom scripts to solve the TL equations and invert the spatial parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental depth profile of ionic charge and chemical potential in operating perovskite layers, directly linking macroscopic impedance to microscopic ion migration. It will establish a new analytical framework for diagnosing spatial inhomogeneities in perovskite-based optoelectronic and neuromorphic devices, advancing beyond phenomenological equivalent circuits.
proposals.novelty: While TL models are standard in fuel cell electrochemistry, their application to spatially resolve ion migration in thin-film perovskite semiconductors is unprecedented. The integration of the 'chemical inductor' concept into a spatially distributed model for depth profiling represents a significant conceptual leap in the interpretation of perovskite EIS.

proposals.title: Designing Fast Ion Conductors via the Chemical Inductor Concept: A Dual Approach to Perovskite Defect Engineering
proposals.motivation: The development of solid-state electrolytes with high ionic conductivity is critical for next-generation batteries and neuromorphic ionic devices. Perovskite oxides are promising candidates, but their ionic transport is often limited by defect trapping and slow kinetics. Recent work on perovskite memristors and the 'chemical inductor' concept reveals that specific defect configurations can produce inductive-like electrical responses, suggesting a pathway to engineer fast ion migration by manipulating internal chemical potentials and relaxation timescales.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the inductive electrochemical signatures observed in perovskite resistive switching devices are directly correlated with specific defect structures (e.g., vacancy clusters or ion channels) that enable fast, correlated ion hopping. By intentionally designing perovskites to maximize this 'chemical inductance,' we can create materials with superior ionic conductivity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of A-site and B-site doped ABO3 perovskites (e.g., SrTiO3, LaAlO3) known for oxygen vacancy conduction. Using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) over a broad frequency range (1 mHz to 1 MHz), we will characterize the materials, fitting the data to equivalent circuit models containing constant phase elements (CPEs) and inductive components. The key analysis will use the derivative of the complex capacitance (dC''/dω) to quantify the chemical inductance, as established in prior work. This metric will be correlated with DC ionic conductivity measurements from impedance and with defect structures characterized by XRD, XPS, and TEM.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, impedance-based design rule for fast ion conductors, moving beyond trial-and-error doping. It will provide a novel functional link between the field of perovskite memristors (electronic switching) and solid-state ionics (ionic conduction). The outcome will be a new class of perovskite materials with tailored defect landscapes for applications in solid electrolytes and bio-inspired electrochemical systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the 'chemical inductor' framework—developed to explain memory and negative capacitance in neuromorphic devices—as an inverse design principle for ionic conductors. This inverts the typical problem: instead of studying conductivity from known structures, we use a targeted electrical signature (inductance) to guide the synthesis of optimal defect structures.

proposals.title: Electrochemical Impedance and Dynamical Voltage Response of Multi-Cation Mixed-Halide Perovskites: Decoding Ionic Hysteresis as a Chemical Inductor System
proposals.motivation: Perovskite solar cells and memristors suffer from performance instability and hysteresis, largely attributed to ion migration. While single-cation/halide systems have been studied, the vastly more complex and tunable multi-cation mixed-halide perovskites, which offer superior optoelectronic properties, present an uncharted landscape of ionic dynamics. Understanding how complex cation/halide mixtures modulate ion migration and hysteretic response is critical for designing stable, high-performance devices and for advancing perovskite-based neuromorphic systems that exploit analog memory.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the specific combination and distribution of multiple A-site cations (e.g., FA+, MA+, Cs+, Rb+) and halides (I-, Br-, Cl-) create a heterogeneous energy landscape for ion migration, which can be modeled as a network of coupled chemical inductors, leading to distinct, tunable hysteretic signatures in voltage-dependent capacitance and impedance.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a series of perovskite thin-film devices (FA_xMA_yCs_zPb(I_aBr_bCl_c)_3) with controlled stoichiometry. Using impedance spectroscopy (IS) across a wide frequency range (1 mHz to 1 MHz) under varying DC bias and illumination, we will extract the voltage-dependent chemical capacitance and recombination resistance. We will apply the 'Chemical Inductor' model and equivalent circuit analysis, specifically looking for inductive loops in the Nyquist plots and anomalous features in Mott-Schottky analysis, to deconvolve the contributions of different ionic species. Dynamical voltage sweeps (cyclic voltammetry, I-V hysteresis loops) will be correlated with the IS data to link transient ionic response to steady-state impedance features.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, impedance-based framework for predicting hysteresis in complex perovskite compositions, directly linking chemical disorder to device-level electrochemical dynamics. It will establish design rules for minimizing detrimental hysteresis in solar cells and for maximizing controllable memory window in perovskite-based resistive switching devices for neuromorphic computing.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the 'Chemical Inductor' concept and advanced impedance models, developed for simpler systems, to decode the coupled ionic dynamics in highly complex, multi-component perovskites—a system whose hysteretic behavior is not a simple superposition of its parts but an emergent property of its compositional complexity.

proposals.title: Modeling Surface Recombination Velocity with an Energy-Dependent Capture Cross-Section: A Density-of-States Weighted Approach
proposals.motivation: Accurate modeling of surface recombination is critical for optimizing perovskite solar cells and photoelectrodes for water splitting, as it directly limits open-circuit voltage and efficiency. Current models, which assume an energy-independent capture coefficient, fail to capture the complex kinetics of real surfaces with broad distributions of trap states. This limitation hinders the predictive design of surface passivation layers and the interpretation of impedance and capacitance spectra in emerging electrochemical systems.
proposals.hypothesis: The effective surface recombination velocity is governed by an energy-dependent microscopic capture cross-section, σ_c(E), convolved with the surface state density-of-states (DOS) and the carrier distribution. This leads to a voltage- and illumination-dependent effective capture coefficient, fundamentally altering the interpretation of recombination kinetics from impedance measurements.
proposals.method: We will adapt the neutron transport formalism, replacing the neutron flux with the carrier (electron/hole) distribution and the microscopic nuclear cross-section with σ_c(E). The effective capture coefficient, C_eff, will be computed via a numerical convolution integral over energy: C_eff = ∫ σ_c(E) * v(E) * g_s(E) * f_c(E) dE, where g_s(E) is the surface state DOS (e.g., Gaussian or exponential), v(E) is carrier velocity, and f_c(E) is the occupation function. This model will be integrated into a drift-diffusion-Poisson solver to simulate current-voltage and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) responses for perovskite devices and semiconductor-liquid junctions, explicitly linking σ_c(E) shape to features in Mott-Schottky and capacitance-frequency plots.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new quantitative framework to deconvolve surface state distributions and their capture kinetics from electrical measurements, moving beyond the constant-coeption approximation. It will directly contribute to the physical interpretation of inductive and capacitive loops in the impedance of perovskite memristors and photoelectrodes, offering a unified description of recombination in resistive switching and energy conversion devices.
proposals.novelty: The explicit introduction of an energy-dependent capture cross-section for surface states is a radical departure from the Shockley-Read-Hall norm. The conceptual and mathematical analogy to neutron transport theory provides a novel, physically-grounded solution to a long-standing problem in semiconductor electrochemistry and device physics.

proposals.title: Impedance Spectroscopy of Synthetic Surface-State Lattices: Engineering Interfacial Chemical Inductance and Capacitance for Programmable Kinetics
proposals.motivation: Interfacial charge transfer in devices like perovskite solar cells is often limited by heterogeneous, disordered surface states, leading to unpredictable kinetics and voltage losses. Pre-2026 work on 'chemical inductors' and Mott-Schottky analysis reveals that surface states can dominate the electrochemical impedance, but control over their energetic distribution and spatial order remains elusive. This proposal addresses the need for deterministic, robust interfaces by constructing a synthetic lattice to replace this disorder.
proposals.hypothesis: A precisely engineered, periodic monolayer of molecular adsorbates on a semiconductor surface will create a synthetic surface-state lattice that acts as a meta-material, decoupling interfacial kinetics from native disorder and introducing programmable, voltage-tunable chemical inductance and capacitance features in the electrochemical impedance spectrum.
proposals.method: We will synthesize tailored phosphonic acid or carboxylic acid monolayers on metal oxide (e.g., TiO2, SnO2) electrodes to create a periodic array of surface states. The electronic structure of the lattice will be designed via molecular orbital engineering of the adsorbates. The interfacial dynamics will be characterized using voltage-dependent electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and intensity-modulated photovoltage spectroscopy (IMVS), interpreted through a modified transmission line model that incorporates the synthetic lattice as a discrete, distributed network of resistive, capacitive, and inductive elements. The analysis will focus on deconvoluting the synthetic lattice's contribution from the bulk and other interfaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new paradigm for interface design, moving from passivation to active programming of charge transfer pathways. It will provide a direct experimental link between atomically precise surface engineering and measurable, tunable features in electrochemical impedance, specifically extending the 'chemical inductor' concept to designed systems. The findings will offer a general strategy to enhance the performance and predictability of optoelectronic and electrochemical devices.
proposals.novelty: While surface passivation is common, the active construction of a periodic surface-state lattice to function as a deterministic kinetic meta-material is a novel concept. This approach uniquely combines molecular self-assembly with the analysis framework of chemical impedance and inductance, going beyond the interpretation of native states to the creation of synthetic ones with designed dynamical properties.

proposals.title: Deconvoluting Recombination Pathways in Photoelectrochemical Cells via Correlated Transient Photovoltage and In-Situ Transient Absorption Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: In photoelectrochemical systems like dye-sensitized or perovskite solar cells, the open-circuit voltage decay is governed by multiple, competing recombination pathways, including charge transfer to redox mediators (e.g., Co(II/III) complexes) and undesired back-electron transfer. Pre-2026, impedance spectroscopy and transient photovoltage are used, but they provide indirect, convoluted signals that cannot uniquely assign kinetic constants to specific chemical species at the interface, hindering rational mediator and interface design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by directly correlating the population dynamics of redox species (e.g., Co(III) generation/decay) with the macroscopic photovoltage transient, we can quantitatively deconvolute the kinetic contribution of the desired mediator regeneration from competing recombination routes, providing validated, species-specific rate constants.
proposals.method: We will develop a custom setup combining high-speed open-circuit photovoltage decay measurements with simultaneous, potential-modulated transient absorption (TA) spectroscopy focused on the electrolyte/electrode interface. A pulsed LED will trigger the photovoltage transient in a sensitized TiO2 or perovskite electrode with a cobalt-based redox electrolyte. A synchronized, wavelength-tunable probe laser will monitor the characteristic absorption bands of the redox species (e.g., Co(III)) in real-time. The correlated voltage and optical decay curves will be analyzed using a dynamical systems model, fitting the data to a equivalent circuit with chemical inductor and capacitor elements, as inspired by Bisquert's previous work, to extract separate kinetic constants for mediator oxidation and recombination.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, in-situ validation of kinetic models for redox mediators, moving beyond fitting of indirect electrical data. It will yield a new experimental benchmark for comparing redox couples and directly identify the dominant loss pathway in operating conditions. This contributes to the foundational understanding of electrochemical systems and neurons by providing a concrete method to dissect voltage dynamics into constituent chemical processes.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious integration of real-time, interface-specific spectroscopy with photovoltage transients directly probes the chemical state of the mediator during the electrical relaxation, a correlation not achieved by standalone electrical or spectroscopic methods pre-2026.

proposals.title: Probing Redox Mediator Dynamics in Perovskite Solar Cells via Open-Circuit Photovoltage Decay: A Chemical Inductor Perspective
proposals.motivation: The performance of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) and light-emitting diodes (PeLEDs) is critically dependent on charge transfer kinetics at the interface with redox-active electrolytes or transport layers. Pre-2026, the field lacks a systematic, non-invasive method to deconvolute the coupled chemical and electronic processes governing charge transfer with different redox mediators, limiting the rational design of stable, high-efficiency devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the open-circuit photovoltage decay (OCVD) transient, when analyzed through the framework of electrochemical impedance and the chemical inductor concept, will reveal distinct, quantifiable signatures for different redox couples, directly linking the mediator's electrochemical properties to the system's capacitive and inductive dynamical response.
proposals.method: We will fabricate methylammonium lead iodide perovskite solar cells with a mesoporous TiO2 electron transport layer. The OCVD method will be applied by illuminating the cell to steady-state and then recording the voltage decay after light cessation. This will be performed systematically with cells employing different redox electrolytes (e.g., I3-/I-, Co(bpy)3^{3+/2+}, ferrocene derivatives). The resulting OCVD curves will be analyzed using a modified equivalent circuit model incorporating a chemical inductance element, derived from Bisquert's prior work. Key parameters, including chemical capacitance, recombination resistance, and the inductive time constant, will be extracted by fitting the voltage decay dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish OCVD as a powerful diagnostic tool for screening redox mediators in perovskite optoelectronics, providing a direct measurement of charge transfer kinetics and chemical charging processes. It will yield a new physical interpretation of voltage transients in dynamically complex electrochemical systems, bridging the gap between impedance spectroscopy and time-domain measurements.
proposals.novelty: While OCVD is known for silicon, its application to perovskite/redox mediator systems through the lens of the chemical inductor model is novel. This approach uniquely disentangles the Faradaic and capacitive contributions to the voltage decay, offering insights beyond standard Mott-Schottky analysis.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Mapping of the Chemical Inductor: Dynamical Surface State Distributions in Perovskite and Metal Oxide Interfaces
proposals.motivation: The performance of electrochemical and optoelectronic devices, such as perovskite solar cells and water-splitting photoanodes, is critically governed by surface and interface states. Pre-2026, techniques like impedance spectroscopy (e.g., Mott-Schottky analysis) reveal capacitive and inductive chemical processes, but they provide an integrated, macroscopic response that obscures the underlying distribution and energetic landscape of these states under dynamic chemical perturbation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the chemical inductor phenomenon and memristive analog memory in perovskite devices arise from a dynamically shifting distribution of surface state energies, which can be systematically mapped and controlled through specific molecular adsorption, creating a quantifiable 'electronic folding landscape'.
proposals.method: We will employ a high-throughput screening platform combining automated drop-casting of a library of organic dyes and surface treatments on perovskite and metal oxide thin films. Each sample will be characterized by a suite of complementary techniques: intensity-modulated photovoltage spectroscopy (IMVS) for surface state lifetime, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) for chemical capacitance and inductance analysis, and Kelvin probe force microscopy (KPFM) for local surface potential mapping. Data will be integrated using a distribution of relaxation times (DRT) analysis and a modified Poisson-Boltzmann solver to deconvolute the energy-dependent density of states (DOS) under each chemical perturbation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, condition-dependent map of surface state distributions, directly linking specific chemical functionalities to changes in the electronic landscape. It will yield quantitative design rules for interface engineering to suppress recombination losses in photovoltaics and enhance charge transfer in electrocatalysis, moving beyond phenomenological equivalent circuit models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting a high-throughput biochemical screening philosophy to solid-state electronic landscapes and quantitatively linking the macroscopic chemical inductor concept to nanoscale, energetically distributed surface states revealed by complementary dynamical probes.

proposals.title: In-Situ Deep Level Transient Spectroscopy of Surface State Dynamics during Molecular Adsorption on Mesoscopic TiO2
proposals.motivation: In dye-sensitized solar cells (DSCs) and photocatalytic systems, the adsorption of molecules fundamentally alters the semiconductor's electronic landscape, creating or passivating surface states that govern recombination and charge transfer. Pre-2026, the energetic distribution and capture kinetics of these dynamically induced states remain poorly quantified, limiting a mechanistic understanding of interfacial disorder. This gap hinders the rational design of high-performance, stable sensitized interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the stepwise adsorption of dye molecules (e.g., N719, D35) or co-adsorbates onto mesoporous TiO2 will induce discrete, quantifiable changes in the surface state density-of-states (DOS), and that in-situ DLTS can correlate specific binding events with the creation or passivation of traps and their associated capture cross-sections.
proposals.method: We will develop a liquid-electrolyte cell compatible with conventional DLTS hardware, enabling in-situ measurement on mesoscopic TiO2 electrodes. A systematic adsorption sequence will be performed by controlling the immersion time in dye solutions of varying concentration. After each adsorption step, temperature-dependent capacitance transients will be recorded under reverse bias following a voltage/light filling pulse. The resulting spectra will be analyzed using the standard DLTS rate-window method and the Langmuir adsorption model to extract trap energy, density, and electron capture cross-section as a function of surface coverage.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative correlation between molecular surface coverage and the electronic trap distribution in a model sensitized semiconductor. It will establish a new spectroscopic tool (in-situ DLTS) for probing interfacial dynamics and deliver crucial parameters for device modeling, linking chemical binding to electronic disorder. The findings will advance the fundamental understanding of recombination pathways in DSCs and related electrochemical systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the real-time, quantitative spectroscopic tracking of surface state evolution during a chemical process (adsorption), a significant advance over static, ex-situ characterizations. This fusion of interfacial electrochemistry with the precision of DLTS for trap parameter extraction is unprecedented in the study of mesoscopic sensitized electrodes.

proposals.title: Mapping Surface State Energetics in Dye-Sensitized and Perovskite Solar Cells via Impedance Spectroscopy and Chemical Inductor Analysis
proposals.motivation: The performance of electrochemical and optoelectronic devices like dye-sensitized solar cells (DSCs) and perovskite solar cells (PSCs) is critically governed by surface states at the semiconductor/dye/electrolyte interface, which mediate recombination and charge transfer. Pre-2026, the energy distribution and density of these states are often treated as static, yet they are dynamically influenced by chemical treatments and dye adsorption, leading to unresolved discrepancies in capacitance-voltage analysis and fill factor losses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematic variations in dye molecular structure and semiconductor surface treatments (e.g., TiCl4, Al2O3 coating, or Lewis base passivation) induce quantifiable shifts in the surface state energy distribution, which can be directly correlated with changes in the chemical inductance and recombination resistance extracted from impedance spectroscopy.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a series of TiO2-based DSCs and perovskite solar cells with controlled surface modifications. Detailed impedance spectroscopy (IS) measurements will be performed under varied bias voltage and illumination. The data will be analyzed using the chemical inductor model, developed by Bisquert's group, to deconvolute surface state capacitance from the chemical capacitance. The energy distribution of surface states (N_ss(E)) will be extracted by fitting the bias-dependent capacitance using a modified Mott-Schottky analysis and a distribution of relaxation times (DRT) algorithm to separate overlapping electrochemical processes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, energy-resolved map of how specific surface modifications alter the interfacial density of states, directly linking molecular design to device electrodynamics. It will establish a robust experimental and analytical framework for diagnosing surface recombination pathways, advancing beyond the standard interpretation of impedance spectra in solar energy conversion systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the chemical inductor concept and DRT analysis specifically to deconvolute the dynamic surface state distribution in operando, moving beyond the assumption of a single discrete surface state energy, which is a significant oversimplification in pre-2026 models of perovskite and DSC interfaces.

proposals.title: Decoding the Chemical Inductor: In-Operando Impedance Spectroscopy of ALD-Engineered TiO2 Interfaces for Dynamic Neuromorphic Optimization
proposals.motivation: The performance of electrochemical and neuromorphic devices (e.g., perovskite memristors, solar cells) is governed by complex, dynamic interfacial processes. Pre-2026, impedance spectroscopy, including Bisquert's 'chemical inductor' concept, is key to analyzing these systems, but it often convolutes bulk and interface effects. A method to isolate and dynamically probe the engineered interface itself is critically needed for rational design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD) to create TiO2 interfacial layers of atomically precise thickness will provide a calibrated testbed, enabling the deconvolution of the interface's specific impedance signature and its direct correlation with ALD process parameters and real-time operational states.
proposals.method: We will fabricate model devices (e.g., metal/TiO2/perovskite or electrolyte) with TiO2 interfaces grown by ALD, varying cycle count (1-50) and post-annealing conditions. In-operando electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) will be performed under voltage sweeps or pulsed stimuli, mimicking device operation. The complex impedance spectra will be analyzed using equivalent circuit models incorporating constant phase elements and inductive loops, explicitly fitting parameters (e.g., interfacial capacitance, charge transfer resistance) to the ALD-defined interface properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a closed-loop platform linking ALD synthesis directly to dynamic electrochemical fingerprints. It will provide a universal methodology to optimize engineered interfaces for specific dynamic responses (e.g., short-term synaptic plasticity in neuromorphic devices or stable charge extraction in photoelectrodes) based on first-principles impedance analysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synergistic use of ALD's thickness control not just for static layer properties, but as a dynamic calibration tool for in-operando EIS, allowing the 'chemical inductor' and other interfacial phenomena to be isolated and engineered with unprecedented precision.

proposals.title: Correlating Surface Chemistry and Bulk Ionic Defect Dynamics in Perovskite Memristors via In-Situ UHV DLTS-XPS
proposals.motivation: The performance and stability of perovskite-based electrochemical devices, such as memristors and solar cells, are critically governed by the interplay between bulk ionic defects and surface chemistry. Pre-2026, techniques like DLTS probe bulk trap states, while XPS analyzes surface composition, but performing these measurements separately introduces ambiguity due to surface contamination and sample history changes.
