A direct, in-situ correlation is essential to establish causal links between surface treatments (e.g., passivation) and the underlying defect spectrum that governs resistive switching and capacitive/inductive phenomena.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific surface chemical states and contaminants (e.g., adsorbed oxygen, carbonates, lead/iodide stoichiometry) directly modulate the activation energy and density of bulk ionic defects responsible for the capacitive and inductive memory effects observed in perovskite resistive switching devices.
proposals.method: We will design and commission a novel UHV system integrating a standard DLTS setup (with a temperature-controlled stage and fast voltage pulse generator) and an XPS spectrometer. The sample, a metal-perovskite-metal stack, will be prepared ex-situ and introduced via a load-lock. We will perform sequential, correlated measurements: first, XPS will characterize the as-introduced surface. Then, without breaking vacuum, DLTS will measure the bulk defect spectrum. The surface will then be modified in-situ via controlled gas dosing or mild ion sputtering, followed immediately by another XPS-DLTS cycle to track correlated changes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first definitive experimental dataset linking surface chemistry to bulk defect parameters in perovskite memristors. It will validate or challenge existing models of surface-mediated defect formation, directly informing passivation strategies. The developed UHV platform will serve as a general tool for correlated bulk-surface analysis in electrochemical and semiconductor materials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, in-situ combination of DLTS (a bulk electrical transient technique) and XPS (a surface chemical analysis technique) within a single UHV environment, enabling unambiguous correlation on the same sample spot, which is not possible with separate, ex-situ measurements.

proposals.title: Direct Observation of Hole Transfer Dynamics in Perovskite Photoanodes via Ultrafast Transient Absorption Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: Understanding the kinetics of hole transfer from a photoanode to the electrolyte is critical for the efficiency of photoelectrochemical water splitting, a key technology for solar fuel production. Pre-2026, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Mott-Schottky analysis, as used by Bisquert's group, provide indirect, frequency-domain information on these interfacial processes but lack the direct, time-resolved view of the initial charge separation event. This knowledge gap limits the rational design of high-performance, stable perovskite-based photoelectrodes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ultrafast transient absorption spectroscopy (TAS) will directly reveal multi-timescale hole transfer dynamics from a metal halide perovskite photoanode to a water oxidation catalyst or electrolyte, and that these measured kinetics will correlate with and explain the chemical inductance and capacitive features observed in low-frequency electrochemical impedance spectra.
proposals.method: We will fabricate perovskite (e.g., CsPbBr3 or MAPbI3) thin-film photoanodes on conductive substrates, with and without common co-catalysts (e.g., NiFeOx). Using a femtosecond pump-probe TAS setup, we will selectively photoexcite the perovskite with a pump pulse and probe the ensuing dynamics from 100 fs to several nanoseconds. Specific spectral signatures of photogenerated holes (oxidized state of the perovskite or catalyst) will be monitored. Kinetic analysis will involve global target modeling to deconvolute processes like hole cooling, trapping, and transfer. The TAS-derived time constants will be directly compared to the characteristic frequencies of chemical inductive loops obtained from EIS measurements on the same devices.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, time-domain map of hole transfer kinetics at the perovskite/electrolyte interface, moving beyond the equivalent circuit modeling of EIS. It will establish a foundational link between ultrafast spectroscopic observables and the electrochemical impedance parameters that define device performance, offering a new physical interpretation for 'chemical inductance' in these systems. This will create a powerful combined methodology for diagnosing and engineering charge transfer bottlenecks in photoelectrochemical cells.
proposals.novelty: While TAS is standard in photovoltaic studies, its application to directly probe the hole transfer step in an operating photoelectrochemical interface, specifically to connect ultrafast dynamics to the low-frequency electrochemical phenomena (like inductance) central to Bisquert's research, is novel. This bridges the fields of ultrafast spectroscopy and electrochemical systems analysis for solar fuel generation.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Recombination-Extraction Trade-off: A Dynamical Impedance Approach to Map the Optimal Surface Area in Perovskite Solar Cells
proposals.motivation: The prevailing design paradigm for high-efficiency semiconductor devices, especially perovskite solar cells, emphasizes maximizing the interfacial surface area for charge extraction. However, this inevitably increases the density of surface recombination centers, creating a fundamental trade-off between photocurrent generation and voltage loss. A quantitative, predictive framework linking nanostructure geometry, surface passivation quality, and final device performance is critically lacking, leading to suboptimal and empirically-driven designs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given set of bulk semiconductor properties and interfacial kinetics, there exists a specific, quantifiable surface area that optimizes the power conversion efficiency by balancing the capacitive extraction pathways against the resistive recombination losses, forming a distinct Pareto front in the voltage-current parameter space.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a series of perovskite thin-film devices with controlled nano- and micro-structuring (e.g., via nanoimprinting or etching) to systematically vary the effective surface area. Independent control of surface recombination velocity will be achieved through graded passivation layers. The key innovation is the use of intensity-modulated photovoltage spectroscopy (IMVS) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to deconvolve the chemical capacitance, recombination resistance, and transport resistance as a function of surface area and light bias. This dynamical systems analysis, inspired by Bisquert's 'chemical inductor' concept, will be correlated with steady-state J-V and photoluminescence quantum yield measurements.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative map (a 'Pareto front') linking device architecture to the recombination-extraction trade-off, moving beyond qualitative guidelines. It will yield a general design principle for tailoring interfacial geometry based on measurable material parameters. The impedance-based methodology will offer a new diagnostic tool for assessing the quality of nanostructured interfaces in optoelectronic devices.
proposals.novelty: The proposal directly challenges the axiomatic 'more surface area is better' rule by treating surface area as an optimizable variable within a constrained dynamical system. It uniquely combines controlled nanostructuring with advanced impedance models, traditionally used for flat interfaces, to decode the non-ideal capacitive and inductive characteristics of complex 3D junctions.

proposals.title: Decoupling Topography from Passivation: ALD-Sculpted TiO2 Interfaces to Quantify Surface-Area-Dependent Voltage Losses in Perovskite Solar Cells
proposals.motivation: In nanostructured perovskite solar cells and photoelectrodes, high interfacial surface area is known to enhance charge extraction but is also suspected to be a primary source of open-circuit voltage (Voc) loss due to increased recombination sites. However, this effect is intrinsically conflated with variations in chemical passivation quality. A method to isolate the pure geometric effect of surface area on voltage is critically needed to guide optimal device architecture design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the reduction in effective interfacial surface area, achieved by conformally thickening an electronically near-perfect ALD-TiO2 layer, will directly and quantifiably increase Voc and reduce recombination, independent of chemical passivation changes.
proposals.method: We will fabricate mesoporous perovskite solar cells with a standard TiO2 electron transport layer. Using atomic layer deposition (ALD), we will first deposit an ultra-thin (2-5 cycles), optimized TiO2 layer to provide a baseline, high-quality chemical passivation. Subsequently, we will create a series of devices where the ALD-TiO2 thickness is systematically increased to 10, 20, and 40 cycles, effectively smoothing the nanostructured interface. Device performance (J-V, EQE) will be characterized alongside electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to extract recombination resistance and chemical capacitance, and Mott-Schottky analysis will monitor interface state density.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental correlation between engineered interfacial topography and Voc, separating geometric from chemical effects. It will yield a quantitative framework for assessing voltage losses in nanostructured devices and establish ALD as a tool for active interface topography modulation, beyond mere passivation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using ALD not just to passivate, but to *sculpt* the interfacial geometry in a controlled manner, enabling a clean test of the long-standing surface area hypothesis for voltage loss that has been impossible to verify with conventional deposition techniques.

proposals.title: Electrochemical Impedance and Dynamical Voltage Analysis of Hybrid Single-Crystal/Porous TiO2 Architectures as a Model Neuronal System
proposals.motivation: In electrochemical systems like photoelectrochemical cells and memristive devices, a fundamental trade-off exists between efficient long-range charge transport and high surface area for reactions or memory effects. This limits device performance and complicates the analysis of their dynamical voltage response. A structured electrode that decouples these functions is needed to study the underlying electrochemical 'neuronal' dynamics without transport losses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hybrid TiO2 architecture, comprising a single-crystalline core for ballistic electron transport and a porous nanocrystalline shell for high interfacial activity, will exhibit a distinct, quantifiable impedance signature characterized by a dominant chemical inductance and a clear separation of transport and surface processes in the voltage dynamics.
proposals.method: We will synthesize vertically aligned, single-crystalline rutile TiO2 nanowire arrays on FTO via hydrothermal methods. A conformal, mesoporous anatase TiO2 shell will be deposited via TiCl4 treatment or electrochemical anodization, ensuring epitaxial connectivity. The complete architectures will be characterized in a three-electrode cell. We will apply Juan Bisquert's impedance models (e.g., the chemical inductor circuit) and analyze voltage transients under modulated illumination or bias to deconvolve the transport time constant of the core from the surface chemical capacitance and recombination resistance of the shell.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a definitive experimental platform to validate and refine impedance models for decoupled transport and surface processes. It will establish design rules for next-generation photoelectrodes and contribute a physical, materials-based analogue for studying voltage-driven dynamical systems relevant to neuromorphic and perovskite resistive switching devices.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in intentionally designing a material system to isolate and study the 'chemical inductor' phenomenon and other interfacial electrochemical dynamics by eliminating the confounding factor of grain boundary transport losses in the primary charge collection pathway.

proposals.title: Decoupling Optical and Electrical Pathways in Mesoscopic Solar Cells: A Core-Shell TiO2 Nanostructure with a Conformal High-Conductivity Shell
proposals.motivation: In mesoscopic photovoltaics, particularly dye-sensitized and perovskite solar cells, a fundamental trade-off exists between achieving optimal light-harvesting through thick, scattering TiO2 films and ensuring efficient charge collection via short, direct pathways. This conflict limits power conversion efficiency. Pre-2026 work has identified surface recombination and tortuous electron transport in nanoparticulate networks as key loss mechanisms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by coating a complex, light-scattering TiO2 core with an ultrathin, aliovalent-doped (e.g., Nb-doped) TiO2 shell via atomic layer deposition (ALD), we can create a dedicated, high-conductivity electron highway. This will decouple the electrical pathway from the optical morphology, passivate surface states, and yield a net positive gain in internal quantum efficiency and overall device performance.
proposals.method: We will synthesize hierarchical, light-scattering TiO2 cores (e.g., mesoporous spheres or aggregates) via hydrothermal methods. A conformal shell of Nb-doped TiO2 will be deposited using ALD, with precise control over thickness (2-10 nm) and doping concentration. The core-shell structures will be integrated as the electron-transport layer in perovskite solar cells. Their performance will be evaluated using current-voltage measurements, impedance spectroscopy (IS), and intensity-modulated photovoltage/photocurrent spectroscopy (IMVS/IMPS). IS will be critically analyzed using the chemical inductor framework and transmission line models to deconvolute transport and recombination resistances.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel materials architecture that directly addresses the optical-electrical trade-off, potentially leading to higher-efficiency devices. It will also yield fundamental insights into charge transport dynamics in hierarchically structured semiconductors, linking nanoscale engineering to macroscopic device performance through advanced electrochemical impedance analysis.
proposals.novelty: While core-shell structures and ALD coatings are known, their application here is novel: using a conductive shell not merely for passivation but as a dedicated, decoupled electrical highway on a complex scattering core. This explicitly applies the 'chemical inductor' concept—where surface states cause inductive loops in IS—to a designed material system aimed at eliminating such losses.

proposals.title: Myelinated Axon-Inspired Core-Shell Nanowire Networks for Enhanced Electron Transport in TiO2 Photoanodes
proposals.motivation: The efficiency of TiO2 photoanodes for solar water splitting is fundamentally limited by poor electron transport and high bulk recombination. While nanostructuring improves surface area, it often exacerbates electron transport losses due to increased grain boundaries and tortuous pathways. A new architectural paradigm is needed to decouple efficient charge collection from the photocatalytic interface.
proposals.hypothesis: Engineering a conductive, degenerately doped SnO2 or metallic nanowire core coated with a mesoporous TiO2 shell will create a 'myelinated axon' analog for photogenerated electrons, drastically reducing transport resistance and recombination by providing a dedicated, high-mobility pathway for electron extraction to the back contact.
proposals.method: We will synthesize core-shell nanowires via hydrothermal growth of SnO2 nanowire cores, followed by sol-gel or ALD deposition of a controlled-thickness, nanoporous TiO2 shell. These will be fabricated into porous photoanode films. The charge transport dynamics will be analyzed using Bisquert's electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) models, specifically looking for a transition from a diffusion-limited to a transmission-line-like response, and quantifying the chemical capacitance and inductive loops associated with charge accumulation and release in the core pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel materials architecture that physically separates the charge generation and collection functions in a photoelectrode. It will yield a new physical model for analyzing engineered transport pathways in porous semiconductors, extending the 'chemical inductor' concept to designed conductive networks, and demonstrate a clear pathway to higher photocurrents at lower bias.
proposals.novelty: The direct bio-inspired analogy of axonal myelination to solve a charge transport problem in photoelectrochemistry is novel. While core-shell structures exist, their design as a continuous, percolating, low-resistance neural-network-like highway specifically to circumvent TiO2's poor conductivity is a non-obvious systems-level solution.

proposals.title: Co-optimization of Dopant Pairs and Surface Chemical Inductors for High-Efficiency Visible-Light TiO2 Photocatalysis
proposals.motivation: Despite decades of research, visible-light photocatalysis with TiO2 remains inefficient due to a fundamental trade-off: bandgap-narrowing dopants often create deep trap states that promote recombination, while surface extraction layers are designed independently. This project addresses the core electrochemical systems problem of coupling bulk electronic structure with interfacial charge transfer kinetics to achieve a genuine performance breakthrough.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by co-designing bulk dopant pairs to minimize deep trap formation and functionalizing the surface with tailored co-catalysts acting as 'chemical inductors' to delay recombination, we can decouple visible-light absorption from charge carrier loss, leading to a substantial increase in overall quantum efficiency.
proposals.method: First, high-throughput DFT (using VASP with HSE06 hybrid functionals) will screen N/TM (e.g., N/W, N/Mo) dopant pairs in anatase TiO2, evaluating bandgap reduction and defect transition levels. The most promising compositions will be synthesized via sol-gel and characterized with XRD and UV-Vis. Second, inspired by the 'chemical inductor' concept, we will functionalize surfaces with ultrathin, conformal layers (e.g., NiOx, CoPi) and analyze the interfacial charge transfer dynamics using intensity-modulated photocurrent spectroscopy (IMPS) and Mott-Schottky analysis to extract the effective chemical capacitance and inductance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new design principle for photocatalysts by treating the bulk doping and surface extraction as a single, co-optimized electrochemical system. It will deliver a concrete materials platform with quantified performance gains and a novel impedance-based framework for diagnosing and engineering the temporal dynamics of photogenerated charges.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit coupling of bulk dopant engineering—aimed at creating a 'clean' bandgap reduction—with the application of the chemical inductor concept to the photocatalyst surface, a systems-level approach not previously applied to classical TiO2 photocatalysis.

proposals.title: Electrochemical Impedance Analysis of Cation-Alloyed TiO2 for Bandgap-Engineered Photoelectrochemical Systems
proposals.motivation: Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is a cornerstone material for photoelectrochemical cells and perovskite solar cells, but its wide bandgap limits visible light absorption. Single-element doping often introduces deep-level defects that act as recombination centers, degrading performance. A new strategy is needed to tune the bandgap predictably while maintaining a high-quality electronic structure.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic co-doping of TiO2 with two or more isovalent or aliovalent cations (e.g., Sn4+/Zr4+, Nb5+/Ta5+) will form a solid solution that enables continuous, linear bandgap tuning analogous to III-V alloys, and the resulting alloyed lattice will suppress deep-level defect states, leading to improved charge separation and transport properties.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of cation-alloyed TiO2 thin films (e.g., Ti1-x-ySnxZryO2) via sol-gel and spray pyrolysis. The bandgap and electronic structure will be characterized by UV-Vis and XPS. The core analysis will employ electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and Mott-Schottky analysis to extract key interfacial parameters—capacitance, donor density, and flat-band potential—as a function of alloy composition. We will model the impedance data using equivalent electrical circuits and the chemical inductor concept to deconvolute bulk and interfacial charge dynamics, linking compositional changes to specific electrochemical system responses.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel materials design principle for metal oxide bandgap engineering, moving beyond single-dopant approaches. It will provide a fundamental impedance-based framework to understand how cation alloying modifies the density of states, charge transfer, and recombination in electrochemical systems, directly informing the design of more efficient photoanodes and perovskite cell transport layers.
proposals.novelty: While alloying is mature in III-V semiconductors, its application to TiO2 for systematic, defect-suppressing bandgap engineering is unexplored. Coupling this materials strategy with advanced EIS and chemical inductor models to diagnose the electrochemical system's response represents a novel, physics-driven synthesis-characterization loop.

proposals.title: Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy of a Co-Engineered Plasmonic-Doped TiO2 System: Probing Resonant Energy Transfer and Charge Dynamics
proposals.motivation: Extending TiO2's photoresponse into the visible spectrum is critical for efficient solar energy conversion, but conventional bandgap narrowing via heavy doping introduces severe recombination losses. Similarly, standalone plasmonic sensitization often suffers from inefficient hot-carrier extraction and parasitic thermalization. A new paradigm is needed that decouples light harvesting from charge transport.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that resonant energy transfer from plasmonic nanoparticles to specific, dopant-induced intragap states in TiO2 will create a highly efficient, panchromatic sensitization pathway, which can be uniquely identified and quantified by its characteristic electrochemical impedance signature, distinct from direct injection or thermal effects.
proposals.method: We will fabricate TiO2 thin films with controlled doping (e.g., Nb, W) to create discrete intragap states, followed by the deposition of size- and shape-tuned Au nanoparticles (NPs). The core analysis will employ voltage-dependent electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and intensity-modulated photovoltage spectroscopy (IMVS) under selective wavelength excitation. We will model the impedance data using equivalent circuits incorporating chemical inductor and transmission line elements to deconvolute the energy transfer pathway's time constants and capacitive contributions from other interfacial processes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new impedance-based framework for diagnosing and quantifying plasmon-to-defect energy transfer, moving beyond optical characterization. It will establish design rules for co-engineering dopant states and plasmonic resonances to optimize charge generation yield, directly contributing to the development of advanced photoelectrodes and neuromorphic ionic devices with light-tunable conductance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the deliberate electronic structure design where dopant states act as targeted acceptors for plasmonic energy, and in applying Bisquert's dynamical electrochemical analysis—typically used for perovskite and chemical inductor systems—to decode the charge transfer kinetics of this hybrid, non-thermal mechanism.

proposals.title: Quantifying Charge Partitioning in a Solid-State Photoelectrochemical Cell via In-Operando Open-Circuit Voltage Decay Analysis
proposals.motivation: Solid-state photoelectrochemical cells (ss-PECs) that combine energy conversion and catalysis in one device are promising for distributed chemical synthesis and sensing, but their efficiency is limited by competitive charge recombination and extraction pathways. Pre-2026, the dynamics of photogenerated hole bifurcation between electrical extraction and surface catalysis in solid-state architectures remain poorly quantified due to the lack of suitable in-operando diagnostics for non-liquid interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: The modified open-circuit voltage decay (OCVD) transient in a monolithic ss-PEC, using a mesoporous TiO2 photoanode and solid organic hole-transport material (HTM), is highly sensitive to the catalytic reaction rate, and its kinetic analysis can quantitatively deconvolve the efficiencies and time constants for charge recombination, electrical extraction, and direct substrate oxidation.
proposals.method: We will fabricate monolithic devices with a mesoporous TiO2 film sensitized for visible light, infiltrated with a solid-state HTM (e.g., spiro-OMeTAD or a polymeric conductor), and coated with a catalyst layer (e.g., IrO2 for water oxidation or a model organic degradation target). The adapted OCVD method will involve recording the voltage decay after illumination cutoff under varying catalytic loads (e.g., with/without substrate, different catalyst loadings) and light intensities. The decay curves will be analyzed using a modified equivalent circuit model, incorporating a chemical inductance element to account for surface state-mediated catalytic charging, and fitted with multi-exponential decay functions to extract characteristic lifetimes.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish OCVD as a powerful, non-invasive tool for diagnosing solid-state photoelectrochemical interfaces, providing the first quantitative framework for partitioning photogenerated holes in a working device. It will deliver design rules for optimizing ss-PECs by revealing the trade-offs between electrical power output and catalytic yield.
proposals.novelty: The fusion of the OCVD technique—common in solar cell analysis—with the 'chemical inductor' concept for catalytic interfaces to diagnose a dual-function solid-state device is novel. Applying this dynamical impedance-inspired analysis to deconvolve three competing charge pathways in a single, non-liquid measurement goes beyond existing electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) studies of similar systems.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Electrochemical Energy Landscape Modulation by Interface Vacancies in Perovskite Memristive Synapses
proposals.motivation: Perovskite-based resistive switching devices show promise for neuromorphic computing due to their analog memory and capacitive/inductive dynamics, mimicking neuronal behavior. However, the role of ionic vacancies at the perovskite/contact interface, a common feature, is poorly quantified in terms of its direct impact on the electronic energy landscape governing charge transport and memory states. Understanding this is critical for designing reliable artificial synapses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific types and concentrations of vacancies at the perovskite/metal (e.g., Au, ITO) interface systematically alter the local electronic density of states and electrostatic potential, leading to predictable changes in the effective Schottky barrier height and the associated capacitive and inductive elements of the device impedance.
proposals.method: We will employ density functional theory (DFT) calculations using the VASP software to model pristine and vacancy-containing interfaces (e.g., I, Pb, or MA vacancies) between MAPbI3 and common contact materials. The electrostatic potential and local density of states will be analyzed to extract changes in the charge injection barrier. These atomistic results will parameterize a compact dynamical model, based on the chemical inductor concept, linking vacancy configuration to impedance spectroscopy features (e.g., low-frequency inductive loops and voltage-dependent capacitance from Mott-Schottky analysis).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles map linking atomic-scale defect chemistry at interfaces to the macroscopic electrochemical impedance observed in perovskite memristors. It will deliver a quantitative framework to interpret impedance spectra of neuromorphic devices and guide interface engineering for specific synaptic dynamics.
proposals.novelty: While interface vacancies are known to affect performance, a direct, calculated connection between specific vacancy structures and the resulting energy landscape for electronic/ionic transport in perovskite neuromorphic devices has not been established, bridging DFT with the chemical inductor model.

proposals.title: Correlating Ionic Vacancy Profiles with Chemical Inductance and Memristive Dynamics in Halide Perovskites via Positron Annihilation Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: Halide perovskites exhibit complex electrochemical dynamics, including memristive switching and the recently proposed 'chemical inductor' effect, which are crucial for neuromorphic and energy applications. These phenomena are widely believed to be governed by ionic vacancy migration and accumulation, yet direct, quantitative, and depth-resolved measurements of vacancy concentration and type under operational voltages are lacking. Establishing a definitive link between specific vacancy profiles and the characteristic electrical signatures (e.g., inductive loops in impedance, analog memory) is a critical, unresolved challenge in the field.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the chemical inductance and analog resistive switching in metal-halide perovskites are directly controlled by the formation of specific vacancy concentration gradients (e.g., V_I, V_Pb) under bias, and that Positron Annihilation Spectroscopy (PAS) can quantitatively map these gradients to correlate with impedance-derived equivalent circuit parameters.
proposals.method: We will fabricate methylammonium lead iodide (MAPbI3) and CsPbBr3 thin-film devices with inert and reactive electrodes. We will perform synchronized electrical and PAS characterization: first, impedance spectroscopy (IS) will be used to extract the chemical inductance and capacitive elements under varying DC bias. Subsequently, samples will be quenched under bias and analyzed using Doppler broadening PAS (DB-PAS) and coincidence Doppler broadening (CDB) to obtain depth profiles of vacancy concentration and chemical identity. The S-W parameter plots from PAS will be quantitatively analyzed to distinguish between cation (Pb) and anion (I/Br) vacancies.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative correlation between electrochemical impedance features (inductance, capacitance) and specific ionic vacancy distributions in operating perovskite devices. It will validate or refine the physical models for chemical inductance and memristive switching, moving beyond phenomenological equivalent circuits. The methodology will establish PAS as a critical tool for diagnosing operational states in electrochemical solid-state systems.
proposals.novelty: While PAS is established for vacancy studies in semiconductors, its application to *operationally biased* perovskite memristors/chemical inductors to deconvolute the ionic origins of dynamical electrical responses is novel. This directly tests the microscopic foundations of Bisquert's 'chemical inductor' model with unprecedented chemical specificity.

proposals.title: Electrochemical Impedance and Voltage-Driven Dynamical Systems in Heavily Rare-Earth-Doped Perovskites: Probing Phase Transformations for Neuromorphic Chemical Inductors
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the field of neuromorphic computing seeks materials with rich electrochemical dynamics to emulate neuronal functions. Perovskite oxides, known for resistive switching and analog memory, offer a promising platform, but their dynamical repertoire is limited by typical doping regimes. Pushing rare-earth (RE) doping to extreme concentrations could induce new perovskite phases or RE titanate formation, potentially unlocking novel voltage-dependent electrochemical responses crucial for advanced synaptic and neuronal emulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that heavy RE doping in perovskite titanates (e.g., SrTiO3) will induce structural phase transformations or secondary phase formation, which will manifest as distinct, tunable features in electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), particularly through the emergence or modulation of chemical inductor behavior and voltage-dependent capacitive elements.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of A-site and/or B-site substituted perovskite oxides (e.g., (Sr1-xLax)TiO3) with RE concentrations (x) systematically pushed beyond typical solubility limits (e.g., x > 0.5) using solid-state and sol-gel methods. Phase purity and transformations will be characterized by XRD and TEM. Thin-film devices will be fabricated, and their electrochemical response will be analyzed using voltage-dependent impedance spectroscopy (up to 1 MHz). The complex capacitance and impedance spectra will be modeled using equivalent circuits and analyzed through the chemical inductor framework and Mott-Schottky formalism to deconvolute interfacial and bulk contributions linked to the new phases.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a materials design principle for inducing phase-based electrochemical complexity in perovskites. It will directly link specific heavy-doping-induced structural changes to quantifiable features in impedance, such as giant or tunable chemical inductance and non-ideal capacitive responses, providing new material systems for building dynamical systems that mimic neuronal voltage processing.
proposals.novelty: While RE doping in perovskites is common, systematically exploring the *electrochemical impedance signature* of phase transformations at ultra-high doping levels for neuromorphic property engineering is unexplored. This bridges the gap between solid-state chemistry phase control and the dynamical systems analysis central to Bisquert's recent work on chemical inductors and perovskite memory devices.

proposals.title: Correlating Chemical Inductive Loops with Carrier Dynamics: A Combined Impedance and Transient Analysis of Perovskite Memristive and Photoelectrochemical Systems
proposals.motivation: The interpretation of impedance spectra in complex electrochemical and electronic systems like perovskite memristors and photoanodes often reveals inductive loops, interpreted as 'chemical inductance' related to slow state filling or ionic processes. However, the physical parameters extracted from equivalent circuit models (e.g., inductance, time constants) remain phenomenological and lack direct validation from independent, time-domain measurements of the same underlying dynamics. This gap limits the predictive power of impedance models for neuromorphic and energy conversion devices.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the characteristic time constants and effective capacitances derived from low-frequency inductive features in impedance spectroscopy directly correlate with, and can be validated by, the relaxation times and charge extracted from transient photovoltage decay (TPV) and transient photocurrent decay (TPC) measurements on the same physical devices.
proposals.method: We will fabricate two device classes central to Bisquert's recent work: (1) volatile perovskite resistive switching devices showing analog memory and (2) metal oxide photoanodes for water splitting. On each device, we will perform voltage-dependent electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) to extract the parameters (R, L, C) of the equivalent circuit containing a chemical inductor. Subsequently, on the same device spot, we will employ small-perturbation TPV and TPC under identical bias conditions, analyzing decay lifetimes via multi-exponential fitting and calculating chemical capacitance from charge extraction. A key analysis will be the direct comparison of the inductive time constant τ_L = L/R from EIS with the dominant TPV/TPC decay time, and the chemical capacitance from C_μ = τ_L/R with that from transient charge analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental cross-validation of the chemical inductor model, strengthening the physical interpretation of low-frequency impedance in mixed ionic-electronic conductors. It will establish a robust protocol for deconvoluting interfacial and bulk state dynamics in perovskite neuromorphic devices and photoelectrodes, directly linking frequency-domain and time-domain analyses.
proposals.novelty: While EIS and transient techniques are used separately, their concerted application on identical devices to validate the specific parameters of the chemical inductor model—particularly linking τ_L to carrier recombination/relaxation times—is novel and moves beyond phenomenological circuit fitting to a dynamically verified framework.

proposals.title: Generalized Transmission Line Model for Impedance Spectroscopy of Mixed Electronic-Ionic Conductors with Coupled Recombination
proposals.motivation: Mixed electronic-ionic conductors (MIECs), such as perovskite semiconductors and memristive oxides, are central to next-generation energy and neuromorphic devices. Their functionality relies on the coupled transport and recombination of electrons and ions, but existing impedance models, like the standard transmission line model (TLM), often treat these processes separately or incompletely. A unified framework is needed to deconvolute the complex impedance signatures of these systems, linking microscopic ionic dynamics to macroscopic electronic responses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the impedance response of a MIEC can be modeled by a generalized TLM where electronic and ionic transport lines are capacitively coupled, and that the recombination rate between electronic and ionic species introduces a characteristic chemical inductance, shaping the low-frequency spectral features.
proposals.method: We will develop a 1D continuum model coupling drift-diffusion equations for electrons and ions with a rate equation for interfacial recombination. This system will be linearized for small-signal AC analysis to derive the governing equations for the generalized TLM. The model will be implemented and solved numerically using finite-element methods in COMSOL Multiphysics, with boundary conditions representing blocking and ohmic contacts. The simulated impedance spectra will be fitted to experimental data from perovskite solar cells and resistive switching devices using a complex nonlinear least squares (CNLS) algorithm to extract transport and recombination parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative tool to separate and quantify electron/ion transport coefficients and recombination rates from a single impedance spectrum. It will establish a direct link between the 'chemical inductor' concept in electrochemical systems and the inductive loops observed in perovskite and memristive devices, offering a unified interpretation for neuromorphic and energy materials.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly incorporating voltage-dependent recombination kinetics between electronic and ionic carriers into the TLM framework, creating a bridge between the 'chemical inductor' model for faradaic processes and the capacitive coupling models used for electronic devices.

proposals.title: Photothermal Impedance Spectroscopy of Perovskite Memristors: Probing Thermal Dynamics of Ion Migration and Recombination
proposals.motivation: In perovskite-based resistive switching devices and solar cells, ion migration and recombination are thermally activated processes critical to device operation and stability. Conventional impedance spectroscopy (IS) uses voltage perturbation, which convolutes electrical and thermal responses, making it difficult to isolate the specific role of temperature fluctuations in device dynamics. A method to directly probe the thermal transfer function is needed to understand and model these complex electrochemical systems.
proposals.hypothesis: Applying a modulated photothermal perturbation, instead of a voltage perturbation, to perovskite devices will generate a measurable thermal impedance spectrum that directly reveals the characteristic time constants and activation energies of ion diffusion and carrier recombination processes.
proposals.method: We will develop a photothermal impedance spectroscopy (PTIS) setup using a focused, intensity-modulated near-infrared laser to induce periodic Joule heating in metal-perovskite-metal structures. The resulting small-signal temperature oscillation will modulate ionic and electronic transport, detected via the device's voltage or current response under a fixed DC bias. The complex thermal impedance, Z_th(ω)=ΔT(ω)/P(ω), will be extracted using a frequency response analyzer. Data will be interpreted using a generalized equivalent circuit model incorporating chemical inductors and constant phase elements, adapted from Bisquert's framework for electrochemical systems, to deconvolve diffusion and recombination rates.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish PTIS as a new diagnostic tool for perovskite electronics and photovoltaics, providing direct access to thermal activation parameters of ionic defects. It will yield a more complete dynamical model of perovskite devices, linking thermal, ionic, and electronic degrees of freedom, which is essential for designing stable memristive synapses and efficient solar cells.
proposals.novelty: While photothermal techniques exist for material characterization, applying them as an impedance spectroscopy method to probe the internal state dynamics of perovskite memristors and solar cells, by treating heat as the input signal, is a novel concept. It extends the 'chemical inductor' framework into the thermal domain.

proposals.title: A Generalized Transmission Line Model for Module-Level Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy of Perovskite Solar Modules
proposals.motivation: Impedance spectroscopy is a powerful tool for diagnosing charge transport and recombination in single perovskite solar cells, often modeled with the transmission line equivalent circuit. However, scaling to commercial modules, where cells are interconnected in series and parallel, remains a challenge. A module-level impedance model is critical for non-destructive, in-situ monitoring of performance losses, degradation pathways, and spatial inhomogeneities in real-world operating conditions, which is a major bottleneck for perovskite PV commercialization.
proposals.hypothesis: The impedance response of a full perovskite solar module can be accurately described by a hierarchical equivalent circuit that integrates single-cell transmission line elements, representing individual cell physics, with network elements that capture the electrical topology and interconnection resistances of the module.
proposals.method: We will develop a generalized impedance model by extending the single-cell transmission line (describing chemical capacitance, transport resistance, and recombination) to a 2D network. The model will explicitly incorporate the module's series-parallel interconnection layout and the resistance of busbars and tabs. We will validate the model by measuring impedance spectra (from mHz to MHz) on custom-fabricated perovskite minimodules with varying numbers of series-connected cells under different illumination and bias voltages. Model parameters will be extracted using complex nonlinear least squares (CNLS) fitting, and spatial information will be inferred by comparing the fitted parameters of individual cell elements within the network.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first physics-based equivalent circuit for interpreting EIS data from complete perovskite solar modules, moving beyond single-cell analysis. It will establish a new diagnostic framework to deconvolute losses from individual cells, interconnections, and sheet resistances, directly informing module design and reliability assessment.
proposals.novelty: While transmission line models for single perovskite cells are established, applying this electrochemical systems approach to model the collective, networked impedance of a full module—treating it as an integrated electrochemical system rather than a simple lumped circuit—is a novel and non-trivial extension of Bisquert's prior work on chemical inductors and Mott-Schottky analysis.

proposals.title: Generalized Impedance Theory for Hole Transport: Deriving the Chemical Inductor and Capacitive Memory in p-Type Perovskite Layers
proposals.motivation: Impedance spectroscopy is a cornerstone for analyzing electron transport and recombination in semiconductors, but its formal dual for hole transport in p-type materials remains underdeveloped. This gap limits the interpretation of key phenomena in emerging p-type perovskite devices, such as resistive switching and photoelectrochemical cells for water splitting, where hole dynamics govern performance. A rigorous theory is needed to decode impedance spectra and link features like inductive loops or giant capacitance directly to hole chemical capacitance, diffusion, and interfacial recombination.
proposals.hypothesis: The impedance response of a p-type semiconductor layer under bias can be formulated as a precise mathematical dual to the well-established electron transport theory, revealing that hole diffusion-recombination generates a characteristic 'chemical inductor' and that voltage-modulated hole accumulation leads to capacitive memory effects analogous to those seen in n-type systems.
proposals.method: We will derive the analytical impedance expression Z(ω) for a p-type layer by solving the continuity and transport equations for holes under small-signal perturbation, directly mirroring the formalism for electrons but with the appropriate sign conventions and boundary conditions for hole current. The model will incorporate voltage-dependent hole chemical capacitance and recombination resistance. We will validate the theory by fitting it to experimental electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data from p-type perovskite (e.g., NiOx-based) devices, including resistive switching memory cells and photoanodes, using complex nonlinear least squares (CNLS) fitting algorithms to extract physical parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified theoretical framework to interpret impedance spectra of p-type semiconductors, explicitly connecting inductive and capacitive features to underlying hole dynamics. It will enable more accurate extraction of transport and recombination parameters from p-type perovskite solar cells, memory devices, and photoelectrodes, advancing their design and optimization.
proposals.novelty: While the electron transport impedance theory is mature, its explicit and formal dual for holes has not been fully articulated and applied to the novel context of perovskite devices exhibiting memory and inductive effects. This proposal directly extends Bisquert's established 'chemical inductor' and capacitance dispersion concepts to the complementary charge carrier.

ideas.title: Dual-Function Solid-State TiO2 Devices with In-Operando Voltage Decay Diagnostics
ideas.description: This research creates a monolithic solid-state device where a mesoporous TiO2 film functions simultaneously as a photoanode for photovoltaic current generation and as a photocatalyst for a target reaction (e.g., water oxidation or pollutant degradation), using a solid organic hole-transport material (HTM) instead of a liquid electrolyte. Crucially, the adapted open-circuit voltage decay (OCVD) method, optimized for solid-state interfaces, is employed as an in-operando diagnostic tool. By analyzing the voltage decay kinetics under different catalytic loads and light intensities, the technique uniquely quantifies the efficiency and dynamics of the bifurcation of photogenerated holes between recombination with electrons in the TiO2, extraction via the HTM for electrical work, and direct injection into the adsorbed substrate for catalysis.

ideas.title: ALD-Engineered Surface Area Modulation for Voltage Recovery in Nanostructured Devices
ideas.description: This research direction combines the surface area hypothesis with atomic layer deposition (ALD) precision to investigate voltage losses in nanostructured optoelectronic devices like perovskite solar cells or photoelectrodes. We will use ALD to deposit ultra-thin, conformal, and electronically 'perfect' TiO2 interfacial layers on high-surface-area nanostructures, then deliberately use further ALD cycles to gradually increase the TiO2 thickness, effectively smoothing the interface and reducing its effective surface area. This allows us to directly correlate the engineered interfacial surface area—while maintaining identical, high-quality chemical passivation—with open-circuit voltage and recombination metrics. The core novelty is using ALD not just for passivation, but as an active tool to sculpt and modulate interfacial topography to isolate the pure effect of surface area on device performance.

ideas.title: Correlated Transient Photovoltage and In-Situ Spectroscopy for Deconvoluting Redox Mediator Kinetics
ideas.description: This approach combines open-circuit photovoltage decay measurements with simultaneous, potential-modulated transient absorption spectroscopy focused on the electrolyte interface. The integrated setup directly monitors the population dynamics of the redox species (e.g., the generation and decay of Co(III) in a cobalt complex system) in real-time during the voltage transient. This correlation uniquely allows researchers to distinguish between photovoltage decay due to desired charge transfer to the target redox mediator and decay caused by competing, undesired recombination routes. The method provides validated, mediator-specific kinetic constants and identifies the dominant recombination pathway for any given redox couple/electrode combination.

ideas.title: Core-Shell Scattering Nanostructures with Integrated Conduction Pathways for Loss-Light Photovoltaics
ideas.description: This project designs and synthesizes hierarchical TiO2 nanostructures with a core-shell architecture, where the complex, light-scattering core is conformally coated with an ultrathin, high-conductivity shell. The shell material, such as aliovalent-doped TiO2, is applied via precise techniques like ALD to passivate surface states and provide a dedicated electron highway, decoupling the electrical pathway from the optically active morphology. This approach directly targets the trade-off between light-harvesting and charge collection, aiming to achieve a net positive gain in power conversion efficiency by ensuring that captured photons are converted to collectable current with minimal loss.

ideas.title: Invert the sensitized solar cell architecture
ideas.description: Design a cell where the QD layer is the primary absorber and transporter, not just a sensitizer.

ideas.title: Plasmonic Energy Transfer to Dopant-Induced Intragap States for Panchromatic TiO2 Sensitization
ideas.description: This research direction proposes a synergistic architecture where TiO2 is lightly doped to create a narrow set of well-defined intragap states, rather than attempting to narrow the entire bandgap. Plasmonic nanoparticles (e.g., Au, Ag) are then integrated not merely for direct plasmonic resonance, but to act as efficient nano-antennas that funnel harvested visible/NIR light specifically into these dopant states via resonant energy transfer. This decouples the light absorption function (handled by the plasmonic component) from the charge separation function (maintained by TiO2's pristine bulk), overcoming the limitations of both standalone doping (charge recombination) and standalone plasmonics (thermal losses). The result is a co-engineered system for broad-spectrum solar energy conversion where the type and placement of dopants dictate the operational wavelength range, tuned by the plasmonic element's resonance.

ideas.title: Dynamic Surface State Spectroscopy via In-Situ DLTS under Dye Adsorption
ideas.description: This research direction combines the systematic variation of surface conditions from Idea A with the precise, quantitative characterization technique of DLTS from Idea B. The core innovation is to perform DLTS measurements in-situ, monitoring the semiconductor surface state distribution as dye molecules adsorb or as a surface treatment is applied. This allows for the correlation of specific molecular binding events or chemical modifications with the creation, passivation, or energetic shifting of electronic trap states, and the simultaneous extraction of their capture cross-sections. The resulting data provides a mechanistic understanding of how interfacial dynamics directly govern electronic disorder in functional semiconductor systems like dye-sensitized solar cells or photocatalytic surfaces.

ideas.sourceTitle: Defect migration in methylammonium lead iodide and its role in perovskite solar cell operation

ideas.title: Mapping the Recombination-Extraction Pareto Front for Optimal Surface Area in Semiconductor Devices
ideas.description: This research rejects the universal 'high surface area is always beneficial' axiom and instead seeks to determine the optimal surface area for a given device. It combines controlled fabrication of nanostructures with precise surface passivation to independently vary surface area and surface recombination velocity. By correlating photoluminescence, transient photovoltage, and device current-voltage characteristics, the work will generate a quantitative map linking surface area to the trade-off between open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current. The outcome will be a design principle for tailoring surface area to maximize power conversion efficiency for specific material properties and interfacial kinetics.

ideas.title: ALD-Engineered TiO2 Interfaces with In-Operando Impedance Spectroscopy for Dynamic Interface Optimization
ideas.description: This research direction combines Atomic Layer Deposition (ALD) for the synthesis of perfectly conformal and tunable TiO2 interfacial layers with advanced electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) performed in operando. The key innovation is using the high-precision, variable-thickness interfaces created by ALD as a calibrated 'testbed' for dynamic EIS analysis. This allows researchers to isolate and model the impedance contributions of the TiO2 interface itself from other device processes, directly linking ALD process parameters (precursor cycles, post-deposition annealing) to real-time interfacial charge transfer kinetics and degradation mechanisms. The synergy creates a closed-loop platform for the rational design and optimization of engineered interfaces in devices like batteries, solar cells, or memristors based on their dynamic electrochemical fingerprint.

ideas.title: Challenge uniform density of states assumption

ideas.title: Synthetic Surface-State Lattices for Programmable Interfacial Kinetics
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond merely inverting the role of surface states to actively constructing them with atomic precision. A designed molecular or atomic layer is assembled at the interface of a semiconductor or electrode, creating a periodic array of surface states with tailored electronic structure. This synthetic lattice acts as a mediating meta-material, providing deterministic pathways for charge extraction or injection while passivating the underlying disordered surface. The system's robustness comes from the decoupling of the engineered function from the native material's surface heterogeneity, enabling predictable and enhanced performance in devices like solar cells or photodetectors.

ideas.title: Adapting Alloying Strategies from III-V Semiconductors for TiO2 Bandgap Engineering
ideas.description: This idea adapts the well-established bandgap engineering strategy from III-V semiconductor technology to the problem of tuning the TiO2 bandgap. In III-V semiconductors, precise bandgap control is achieved by forming ternary or quaternary alloys (e.g., InGaAs) through cation substitution, enabling continuous tuning of optical and electronic properties. The adapted research idea proposes to investigate the creation of analogous 'cation alloys' in TiO2 by systematically co-doping with two or more foreign cations (e.g., Sn and Zr, Nb and Ta) to form a solid solution. The goal is to shift the TiO2 bandgap predictably and continuously for optimal visible light absorption, while the alloyed, more homogeneous lattice environment may help suppress the deep-level defect states that often plague single-element doping, thereby improving photocatalytic efficiency.

ideas.title: Quantify chemical capacitance's role in recombination kinetics
ideas.description: Directly link the measured chemical capacitance to specific electron recombination pathways and rates in the nanostructure.
ideas.sourceTitle: Chemical capacitance of nanostructured semiconductors: its origin and significance for nanocomposite solar cells

ideas.title: Co-optimized dopant and charge extraction systems for efficient visible-light TiO2 photocatalysts
ideas.description: This project moves beyond simple bandgap narrowing to co-design the dopant and the charge extraction interface. First, high-throughput DFT calculations will screen for dopant combinations (e.g., nitrogen with a transition metal) that reduce the bandgap while minimizing deep trap state formation. The most promising candidates will then be synthesized, and their surfaces will be functionalized with tailored co-catalysts or ultrathin, conductive overlayers to provide immediate pathways for hole or electron extraction. The final performance metric is the overall quantum efficiency under visible light, ensuring that improved absorption directly translates to higher catalytic activity.

ideas.title: Energy-Dependent Surface Recombination via a Density-of-States Weighted Capture Model
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption of an energy-independent electron capture coefficient at semiconductor surfaces by introducing a model where the coefficient is explicitly energy-dependent. The analogy is drawn from nuclear physics, specifically neutron transport theory, where the macroscopic capture cross-section is derived from an energy-dependent microscopic cross-section convolved with the neutron flux. Adapting this solution, we propose to model surface state recombination by defining an energy-dependent microscopic capture cross-section for individual states. The effective capture coefficient for the conduction or valence band is then computed by integrating this cross-section over the density of surface states and the relevant carrier energy distribution, creating a more physically accurate and predictive model for recombination velocity and device performance.

ideas.title: Dual: study surface states for fuel reduction

ideas.title: Hybrid single-crystal core / porous nanocrystalline shell TiO2 architectures for optimized transport and surface area
ideas.description: This approach synthesizes vertically aligned single-crystalline TiO2 nanowire or nanosheet arrays on conductive substrates to serve as a direct electron highway, eliminating grain boundary losses in the primary charge transport direction. A conformal, mesoporous nanocrystalline TiO2 layer is then grown epitaxially or via careful processing on these cores, creating a high-surface-area shell for dye loading or catalytic reactions. The design decouples the requirements for long-range electron transport (handled by the single-crystal core) from the need for high surface area (provided by the shell). Interface engineering between the core and shell minimizes recombination, creating a system that overcomes the fatal flaw of dense single-crystal films while preserving their key electronic advantage.

ideas.title: Mapping the Electronic Landscape: High-Throughput Spectroscopy for Surface State Distributions Under Chemical Perturbation
ideas.description: This research adapts the high-throughput perturbation screening approach from structural biology (e.g., HDX-MS for protein folding landscapes) to the study of semiconductor surface states. The analogous solution involves systematically probing a system's energetic landscape under varied conditions to build a dynamic map. Adapted here, the idea is to investigate how surface state energy distribution changes with different dye adsorption or semiconductor treatments by employing a suite of complementary surface-sensitive spectroscopic and scanning probe techniques. These techniques will be applied in a high-throughput manner to a systematically varied library of surface modifications, generating a comprehensive, condition-dependent map of the electronic 'folding landscape' of the semiconductor surface. This will reveal how specific chemical perturbations selectively fill, create, or shift surface states, providing design rules for optimized interfaces in photovoltaics and catalysis.

ideas.sourceTitle: Determination of rate constants for charge transfer and the distribution of semiconductor and electrolyte electronic energy levels in dye-sensitized solar cells by open-circuit photovoltage decay method.

ideas.title: Engineer TiO2 Photoanodes with a 'Neural Network' of Conductive Pathways
ideas.description: This idea applies the neurobiological principle of axonal myelination to improve charge transport in TiO2 photoanodes. In neuroscience, myelin sheaths insulate neuronal axons to prevent signal loss and accelerate transmission. The adapted solution is to engineer a core-shell nanomaterial where a conductive core (e.g., a metal or degenerately doped semiconductor nanowire) is coated with a thin, nanoporous TiO2 shell. The core acts as a dedicated, low-resistance 'myelinated axon' for rapid electron extraction, minimizing travel through the TiO2 bulk where recombination is high. The surrounding TiO2 shell remains the active photocatalytic layer for water oxidation. This architecture aims to fundamentally separate the charge generation and collection functions, thereby reducing recombination losses and enhancing overall electron mobility and photocurrent efficiency.

ideas.title: Multi-modal UHV system combining DLTS with surface spectroscopy for correlated bulk-surface defect characterization
ideas.description: This approach integrates deep-level transient spectroscopy (DLTS) with surface-sensitive X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) within a single ultra-high vacuum (UHV) chamber. The system enables the simultaneous, correlated measurement of bulk electronic trap states via DLTS and the chemical composition/electronic states of the surface via XPS on the same sample without breaking vacuum. This direct correlation overcomes the fundamental limitation of DLTS as a bulk technique, allowing researchers to definitively link specific surface treatments or contaminants to changes in the underlying bulk defect spectrum. The result is a robust, multi-modal characterization platform for understanding the interplay between surface states and bulk defects in semiconductors and thin-film materials.

ideas.title: Decoupling Electronic and Structural Contributions to Surface State Modulation via In-Situ Multi-Modal Spectroscopy
ideas.description: This study investigates surface state energy distributions by employing a tightly integrated, in-situ multi-probe system to isolate the electronic effects of dye adsorption or semiconductor treatments from structural and chemical artifacts. The experimental setup combines scanning tunneling spectroscopy (STS), non-contact atomic force microscopy (nc-AFM), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) within a single ultra-high vacuum chamber, allowing sequential or co-located measurements on the same sample region without atmospheric exposure. This approach directly correlates changes in the density of states with concurrent atomic-scale structural rearrangements and chemical state evolution under controlled conditions. The result is a robust, mechanistic understanding of whether surface state changes are driven by direct electronic coupling, surface reconstruction, or interfacial chemical reactions.

ideas.title: Optimal Surface Area for Charge Collection: Borrowing from Catalytic Selectivity
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that high surface area is always beneficial in optoelectronic devices by drawing an analogy from heterogeneous catalysis. In catalysis, simply maximizing surface area can sometimes reduce selectivity by promoting side reactions; the solution is to architect the catalyst surface to favor the desired pathway. Adapted to device physics, the idea is to investigate whether minimizing or strategically redesigning surface area in specific regions of a device (e.g., near the charge-separation junction) can reduce surface recombination losses and improve open-circuit voltage. This would involve designing and testing device architectures where light-absorbing, high-surface-area scaffolds are physically or electronically isolated from critical transport interfaces, or where surface passivation is applied selectively, aiming to optimize the trade-off between light absorption/charge generation and voltage-preserving charge collection.

ideas.title: The Amorphous Advantage: Borrowing from Metallic Glass Design for TiO2 Optimization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that crystalline anatase is always the optimal phase of TiO2 for charge transport and interface properties. It draws an analogy from metallurgy, where the development of metallic glasses (amorphous metals) overturned the similar assumption that crystalline order was always superior for mechanical properties. The analogous solution involved discovering specific alloy compositions and rapid cooling techniques to stabilize amorphous phases, which offered unique benefits like high strength and corrosion resistance. Adapted to TiO2 research, this proposes a systematic investigation into non-equilibrium synthesis techniques—such as ultra-fast quenching, pulsed laser deposition, or low-temperature chemical methods—to create and stabilize amorphous or other non-anatase phases. The research would aim to identify the processing windows and potential chemical modifiers (dopants) that yield TiO2 with potentially superior isotropic electronic transport, fewer recombination sites at grain boundaries, or more tunable surface states for specific semiconductor interfaces, thereby testing the hypothesis that disorder or alternative phase structures could be purposefully engineered for enhanced performance in optoelectronic and energy devices.

ideas.title: Direct Experimental Mapping of Energy-Dependent Surface Recombination Kinetics via Coupled Spectroscopic-Electrical Probing
ideas.description: This approach directly addresses the parameterization problem by designing an experiment to measure the energy-dependent capture coefficient, rather than just modeling it. It combines a tunable excitation source, such as a monochromatic light source or a deep-level transient spectroscopy (DLTS) setup with variable filling pulses, with a time-resolved detection method sensitive to carrier recombination. This allows for the direct experimental construction of a capture cross-section versus energy profile for surface states, providing the necessary physical constraint for subsequent device modeling. The resulting data transforms the unknown function into a measured quantity, enabling physically accurate, predictive simulations of surface recombination.

The core-shell design, featuring a plasmonic metal core (Au) and a thin, conformal SiO2 or Al2O3 shell, maintains the critical plasmonic near-field enhancement and light-scattering benefits while providing exceptional chemical and photochemical stability. This architecture ensures the composite material retains its enhanced light absorption and charge generation properties over extended operational periods, overcoming the primary failure mode of traditional plasmonic-TiO2 systems. The focus is on synthesizing and optimizing this protected interface to achieve both high performance and long-term durability in applications like water splitting or pollutant degradation.

ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept by drawing an analogy from RF antenna engineering. The analogous solution involves separating the functions of wave capture (antenna) and energy conversion (receiver), connected by an efficient transfer mechanism. Adapted to the plasmonic-TiO2 context, the research would design metal nanoparticles as resonant optical nano-antennas, specifically engineered to capture visible light where TiO2 is weak. The core challenge becomes optimizing the energy transfer pathway from the plasmonic antenna to the TiO2 receiver. This involves tailoring nanoparticle shape, size, and coating, and precisely controlling their integration with the TiO2 nanostructure (e.g., core-shell, embedded, or decorated configurations) to maximize near-field coupling or hot electron injection, thereby significantly enhancing the overall photoconversion efficiency beyond simple scattering effects.

ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from Biophysical Chemistry, where Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC) characterizes molecular interactions by measuring heat flow from controlled titrations. The analogous problem in surface science is determining the energy distribution and kinetics of electronic surface states. The adapted method involves applying controlled optical or electrical perturbation pulses to a material sample to selectively fill surface states, while simultaneously monitoring the sample's transient thermal response with nanocalorimetric precision. The resulting thermogram, analogous to an ITC binding curve, would be analyzed to extract the enthalpy spectrum of state filling/emptying, the density of states, and capture cross-sections, providing a novel calorimetry-based complement to electrical DLTS for comprehensive surface state characterization.

ideas.title: Macroporous TiO2 Photonic Crystal Monoliths for Integrated Photonic-Chemical Reactors
ideas.description: This research direction fabricates centimeter-sized, mechanically stable TiO2 monoliths that incorporate a 3D photonic crystal lattice into their macroporous architecture. The design creates a complete photonic bandgap within the monolith, allowing for unprecedented control over light trapping, scattering, and guided propagation through the entire macroscopic volume. Simultaneously, the interconnected, tailored porosity enables efficient, convective fluid transport, directly exposing reactants to the photocatalytically active, light-managed surfaces. This results in a unified, scalable device that functions as both an optical cavity and a chemical reactor, dramatically enhancing photocatalytic efficiency for applications like solar water purification or chemical synthesis under concentrated or diffuse light.

ideas.title: Adapting Phononic Crystal Bandgap Engineering to TiO2 Photonic Films
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept by drawing an analogy from acoustics. The analogous field is Acoustics and Vibration Control, where phononic crystals are engineered with periodic variations to create bandgaps that control mechanical wave propagation, a solution used for sound insulation and vibration damping. We adapt this by applying the core design principle—using a carefully engineered periodic nanostructure to create forbidden bands for wave propagation—to the optical properties of TiO2 films. The adapted research idea involves designing and fabricating TiO2 films where the nanoscale architecture (e.g., a periodic array of pores or pillars) is explicitly engineered to mimic a photonic crystal with targeted photonic bandgaps. This would allow for advanced light management by selectively enhancing light-matter interaction at specific wavelengths critical for applications such as photocatalysis, photovoltaics, or sensing, moving beyond simple scattering or reflection control to precise spectral engineering.

ideas.title: Bio-Templated TiO2 Photonic Sponges for Multi-Chromophore Cascade Energy Transfer
ideas.description: This research direction employs intricate biological templates (e.g., butterfly wing scales, diatom frustules, or plant vascular networks) to synthesize TiO2 nanostructures with complex, multi-scale photonic architectures. These bio-templated 'photonic sponges' are not merely for light trapping but are specifically engineered to host a spatially organized cascade of multiple sensitizer chromophores or quantum dots. The unique morphology provides distinct micro-environments and energy gradients, facilitating sequential Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) from broader-bandgap to narrower-bandgap sensitizers, ultimately funneling excitons to the TiO2 surface or co-catalysts. This approach decouples and optimizes the processes of broadband light absorption and charge injection, leveraging TiO2's stable wide bandgap while achieving panchromatic solar energy conversion without relying on bulk doping.

ideas.title: Decoupling Coupled Hole Transfer Pathways via Quantum Interference Control
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that intrinsically quantum-mechanically coupled charge transfer pathways are fundamentally inseparable. Drawing an analogy from quantum optics, where coherent control techniques solve the analogous problem of indistinguishable quantum pathways, we propose a method to decouple direct and indirect hole transfer. In quantum optics, phase-locked laser pulses are used to control interference between pathways, enabling their indirect separation. Adapted to solid-state or molecular hole transfer, this involves applying phase-controlled perturbations (e.g., tailored ultrafast laser pulse sequences or coherent AC electronic fields) to the donor-acceptor system. These perturbations differentially affect the phase of the wavefunction propagating along the direct versus indirect (e.g., bridge-mediated) tunneling paths. By systematically varying the control parameters and measuring the resulting interference in the hole transfer yield or rate, one can reconstruct the individual contributions and coupling parameters of the two paths, effectively decoupling them through analysis of their controlled interference, even when they are intrinsically coupled in the static system.

ideas.title: Inkjet-Printed, Hierarchically Structured TiO2 Monoliths for Programmable Photonic and Fluidic Architectures
ideas.description: This research direction combines the fabrication of macro-scale, porous TiO2 monoliths with the precision of graphic arts inkjet printing to create a new class of hierarchically structured devices. The core innovation is using multi-material, nanoparticle-based inks to print sacrificial templates and functional dopants directly within a TiO2 precursor gel before its consolidation into a monolithic ceramic. This process allows for the programmable definition of internal channels, graded porosity, and regional variations in electronic or catalytic properties within the final centimeter-sized solid. The resulting monolithic devices integrate engineered light-concentrating nanostructures, fluidic networks for reactant delivery, and patterned surface chemistries, enabling advanced applications in monolithic photocatalytic reactors, integrated optofluidic solar cells, and multi-functional environmental sensors that are impossible with either top-down patterning or bulk material synthesis alone.

ideas.title: Active Pathway Interrogation via Gated, Spatially Resolved Hole Tracking
ideas.description: This research designs a molecular or quantum dot heterostructure with two engineered hole transfer pathways to a common acceptor. A key innovation is the incorporation of a photo-isomerizable molecular bridge that acts as a light-activated gate for the indirect path. Concurrently, single-molecule electro-optical sensing pinpoints the hole's position with nanoscale resolution. By toggling the gate and observing real-time spatial charge dynamics, the experiment can directly measure how blocking one path modulates flux through the other, providing unambiguous, quantitative evidence of coupling or its absence.

ideas.title: Apply Mechanical Modal Analysis to Electrochemical Systems via Impedance Spectroscopy
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology of mechanical modal analysis to electrochemistry. In structural dynamics, a known vibrational force is applied across frequencies to identify a system's natural modes. Analogously, in electrochemistry, a small AC potential perturbation across a frequency range (EIS) serves as the excitation, and the current response is measured. The adapted solution involves developing analysis frameworks to decompose the resulting Nyquist or Bode plots not just into equivalent circuit elements, but into distinct 'electrochemical modes'—quantifiable dynamic signatures for individual charge transfer, adsorption, or mass transport processes. This provides a dynamic, frequency-resolved 'modal map' of the electrochemical interface, offering deeper insight into coupled processes and their timescales than static equivalent circuit fitting alone.
